content_sections.I Introduction: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) provide an effective description of quarks and gluons inside a light-travelling nucleoncitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. They play an essential role in calculating hardronic cross sections involving the nucleonscitebib.bib3, and their uncertainties from phenomenological extractions have been one of the major sources of errors in the theoretical predictions at Large Hadron Collider (LHC) and other hadron facilities. Thus, a precise knowledge of PDFs is very important both for the accurate tests of the Standard Model (SM) and for the search of new physics beyond the SM. On the other hand, the densities of partons in the nucleon provide direct information on its intrinsic properties such as the origin of the nucleon spin and mass, as well as the role of sea quarks for various physical quantitiescitebib.bib4. However, directly calculating parton physics from first principles of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) has been a difficult task. A review on various efforts of doing so can be found in Ref. citebib.bib5. The proposal of the large-momentum effective field theory (LaMET) has been an important step toward meeting the challenge citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. So far, LaMET has been widely used in calculating quark isovector distribution functionscitebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, generalized parton distributionscitebib.bib25, bib.bib26, distribution amplitudes (DAs)citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, and transverse-momentum-dependent distributionscitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. LaMET suggests to calculate time-independent physical distributions in a finite momentum nucleon, which are Euclidean observables. Such finite-momentum quantities can then be matched to light-front (LF) parton properties using effective field theory techniquescitebib.bib33. For example, for the collinear quark distributions, it has been suggested to first compute a Euclidean space correlation (or quasi-light-front (quasi-LF) correlation) on the lattice, hвҖӢ(z,Pz)=вҹЁP|OОіtвҖӢ(z)|Pвҹ©,в„Һрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§quantum-operator-productрқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқ‘ғdisplaystyletildeh(z,Pz)= P|Ogammat(z)|P, where |Pвҹ©ketрқ‘ғ|P is the nucleon state with a large momentum Pрқ‘ғP and the non-local operator is OО“вҖӢ(z)=ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢО“вҖӢUвҖӢ(0,z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z),subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§ВҜрқң“0О“рқ‘Ҳ0рқ‘§рқң“рқ‘§displaystyle OGamma(z)=barpsiGamma U(0,z)psi(z), where ПҲрқң“psi, ПҲВҜВҜрқң“barpsi denote the bare quark field, О“О“Gamma is a Dirac structure, and UвҖӢ(0,z)=expвҒЎ(вҲ’iвҖӢgвҖӢвҲ«0zрқ‘‘zвҖІвҖӢAzвҖӢ(zвҖІ))рқ‘Ҳ0рқ‘§рқ‘–рқ‘”superscriptsubscript0рқ‘§differential-dsuperscriptрқ‘§вҖІsubscriptрқҗҙрқ‘§superscriptрқ‘§вҖІU(0,z)=exp(-igint0zdzprimeAz(zprime)) is the Wilson link along the direction zрқ‘§z, where AОјsuperscriptрқҗҙрқңҮAmu is the gluon gauge potential. After renormalizing hв„Һtildeh properly and matching it to some continuum scheme such as dimensional regularization and (modified) minimal subtraction (MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS), one obtains the so-called quark quasi-PDF via a Fourier transform, from which the quark PDF can be extracted through perturbative QCD matching. Renormalizing the quasi-LF correlation hв„Һtildeh under lattice regularization is a non-trivial task. To see this, let us take, as a simple example, the matrix element of OО“вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§OGamma(z) in an off-shell quark state |qвҹ©ketрқ‘һ|q with large Euclidean momentum p2superscriptрқ‘қ2p2 (actually an off-shell truncated GreenвҖҷs function). It has the following form at 1-loop levelcitebib.bib34, вҹЁOО“вҖӢ(z)вҹ©=О“вҖӢ(1+ОівҖӢg2вҖӢlogвҖӢ(z2/a2)вҲ’mвҲ’1вҖӢza+вҖҰвҖӢвҖҰ),delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§О“1рқӣҫsuperscriptрқ‘”2logsuperscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқ‘Һ2subscriptрқ‘ҡ1рқ‘§рқ‘ҺвҖҰвҖҰdisplaystyle OGamma(z)=Gammaleft(1+gamma g2textrmlog(z2/a2)-m-1fracza+вҖҰвҖҰright), where gрқ‘”g is the bare gauge coupling and aрқ‘Һa is the lattice spacing. Unlike the coefficient of the logarithm Оірқӣҫgamma, the coefficient of the linear divergence mвҲ’1subscriptрқ‘ҡ1m-1 can be sensitive to the details of the fermion and gauge actions on the lattice as those actions themselves define the lattice regularization. The linear-divergence term is proportional to the Wilson link length z/aрқ‘§рқ‘Һz/a in lattice units, and it can be exponentially large at large zрқ‘§z or small aрқ‘Һa, when higher-order corrections are included. Thus, the linear divergence effect has to be removed before the lattice calculated quasi-PDF can be extrapolated to the continuum limit aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0arightarrow 0 and eventually matched to the continuum-scheme PDF. Theoretical studies so far have shown that the quasi-PDF operator is multiplicatively renormalizablecitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38 in a continuum theory. On the lattice, due to non-commutativity of the limit zвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘§0zto 0 and aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0ato 0, it has been suggested recentlycitebib.bib39 to use a hybrid scheme to renormalize the large and short distance correlations separately, which has the advantage of avoiding certain discretization effects and undesired infrared effects introduced in the renormalization stage. At small zрқ‘§z where a short distance expansion is valid, one can use various ratio schemes, such as dividing by an off-shell quark matrix element of the quasi-PDF operator in the regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) schemecitebib.bib38, bib.bib40, bib.bib34 or by a hadron matrix element in the rest frame hвҖӢ(z,Pz=0)в„Һрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§0tildeh(z,Pz=0)citebib.bib41, bib.bib42. At large distance, one can directly remove the Wilson line self-energy effect by subtracting the power divergencecitebib.bib43, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. Apart from using the RI/MOM factor or the rest-frame hadron matrix element, other methods have been suggested to extract the linear divergence, including Wilson loopcitebib.bib43, bib.bib27, vacuum expectation values вҹЁOО“вҖӢ(z)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§ OGamma(z)citebib.bib44, and gauge fixed Wilson linkcitebib.bib8, and so oncitebib.bib39. However, numerically subtracting the linear divergence is an extremely delicate exercise. First, linear divergence could be sensitive to computational systematics in lattice calculations. In data, there may be slight differences between two matrix elements with the same linear divergence. These differences may lead to a failure of the suggested methods, especially for small lattice spacings. Second, some of the renormalization factors have other problems. For example, the leading contribution to the vacuum expectation value of вҹЁOО“вҖӢ(z)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§ OGamma(z) at short distance vanishes and therefore, it is numerically very challenging to obtain the relevant linear divergence. Finally, as we shall see, linearly-divergent chiral symmetry breaking effects for Wilson fermions may render the linear divergence non-universalcitebib.bib45. To understand better the effects of the linear divergence in LaMET applications, we study systematically the linear divergences of matrix elements for sets of lattice data, generated with different lattice actions. We propose a self-renormalization method to eliminate all divergences and discretization errors when data for several different lattice spacings are available. The idea of this method is to extract the renormalization factor and the residual contribution directly from the matrix element we want to renormalize, without using an additional matrix element for renormalization. We then match the empirically renormalized matrix elements to those in the continuum. Our method is largely model-independent, and each term of our fitting functions is motivated by physics considerations. Tests show that our method works well for all data sets considered, which include matrix elements calculated with the lattice spacings from 0.030.030.03 fm to 0.120.120.12 fm using the valence clover and overlap actions on the MILCcitebib.bib46 Nf=2+1+1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘“211Nf=2+1+1 and RBCcitebib.bib47 Nf=2+1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘“21Nf=2+1 configurations. We also find that the method works for matrix elements after applying smearing which is needed to improve the statistical precision. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. We present the theory of perturbative renormalization in Sec. . The definition of the matrix elements we calculate and the simulation setup can be found in Sec. . In Sec. the linear divergence is analysed for the different ensembles. Sec. presents our strategy to self-renormalize a matrix element and collects the results for the OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix elements in different states and calculated with different valence fermion actions. Sec. discusses other fitting options, which mainly differ in the treatment of higher-order terms. In Sec. ,, and, we only analyze matrix elements without HYP smearing. In Sec. , we extend our method to HYP smeared cases. In Sec. , we test the linear divergence in some vacuum state matrix elements, including Wilson loop, quasi-PDF operator, and gauge fixed Wilson link, for the HYP smeared cases. In Sec. , we summarize the results and discuss issues to be addressed in further studies.
content_sections.II Renormalization in perturbation theor: According to the standard renormalization in local quantum field theoriescitebib.bib48, the renormalization of the matrix elements of an operator does not depend on the external states but is only related to the short-distance property of the operator itself. Therefore, one can study the renormalization property of the operator in perturbative GreenвҖҷs functions, or off-shell quark and gluon matrix elements. For LaMET applications to PDFs, we are interested in the non-local operator OО“вҖӢ(z)=ПҲВҜвҖӢвҖӢО“вҖӢUвҖӢ(0,z)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§ВҜрқң“0О“рқ‘Ҳ0рқ‘§рқң“рқ‘§OGamma(z)=barpsiGamma U(0,z)psi(z) in Eq. . There are two types of divergences: The linear divergence associated with the Wilson link (its self-energy) and the logarithmic divergences associated with the renormalization of the vertices involving the Wilson line and light quark. The renormalization is multiplicative, and only the linear divergence has a (linear) zрқ‘§z dependence. Therefore the renormalized operator iscitebib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39 OО“вҖӢ(z)R=ZOвҲ’1вҖӢeОҙвҖӢmВҜвҖӢzвҖӢOО“вҖӢ(z),subscriptрқ‘ӮО“subscriptрқ‘§рқ‘…superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯ1superscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘§displaystyle OGamma(z)R=ZO-1edeltabarmzOGamma(z), where eОҙвҖӢmВҜвҖӢzsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡрқ‘§edeltabarmz contains the linear divergence and ZOsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘ӮZO the logarithmic ones. ОҙвҖӢmВҜрқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡdeltabarm is not uniquely defined apart from the linear divergence, introducing a subtraction scheme dependence which affects the zрқ‘§z-dependence of the renormalized operatorcitebib.bib39. The linearly-divergent mass renormalization can be calculated in perturbation theory. At one-loop order, the result is independent of lattice actioncitebib.bib43, ОҙвҖӢmВҜ=2вҖӢПҖ3вҖӢaвҖӢ(Оұs+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(Оұs2)+вҖҰ). рқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡ2рқңӢ3рқ‘Һsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ’Әsuperscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2вҖҰdisplaystyledeltabarm=frac2pi3a(alphas+cal O(alphas2)+. . . ) . The energy scale of Оұssubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ alphas can be chosen as 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a to match the lattice results, ОұsвҖӢ(1/a,ОӣQCD)=2вҖӢПҖb0вҖӢlnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD)],subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 1рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCD2рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ01рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDdisplaystylealphas(1/a,Lambdarm QCD)=frac2pib0ln[1/(aLambdarm QCD)] , where b0=11вҲ’23вҖӢnfsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ01123subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“b0=11-frac23nf (we will take nfsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“nf=3) is the QCD ОІрқӣҪbeta function at one-loop order with nfsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“nf species of fermions. ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD is the non-perturbative QCD scale. Including higher-orders in the ОІрқӣҪbeta function will lead to a more complicated expression. Different choices of energy scale amount to including high-order corrections. ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD and higher-order Оұssubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ alphas terms in ОҙвҖӢmВҜрқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡdeltabarm also depend on the lattice action. The perturbation theory does not converge due to infrared renormalons, see from examplecitebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52. The existence of the renormalons signals a non-perturbative term in ОҙвҖӢmВҜрқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡdeltabarm which is independent of aрқ‘Һa, ОҙвҖӢmВҜ=mвҲ’1вҖӢ(a)/aвҲ’m0,рқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘ҡ1рқ‘Һрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҡ0deltabarm=m-1(a)/a-m0 , where the minus sign is just a convention. The uncertainty in summing the perturbation series to get mвҲ’1вҖӢ(a)subscriptрқ‘ҡ1рқ‘Һm-1(a) is compensated by the same uncertainty in the non-perturbative m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0, leaving the total independent of the renormalon uncertainty. Additional uncertainty in m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 comes from the subtraction scheme, or equivalently from the matching to the continuum scheme. To reduce the subtraction scheme dependence, we require the renormalized lattice correlation function to be consistent with the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS result from continuum perturbation theory at short-distances zрқ‘§z. To be more concrete, we determine m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 by matching the lattice result to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS one within a window aвүӘz<zSmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Ҷall z<zS, where zS<1/Ојsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Ҷ1рқңҮzS<1/mu is the point beyond which perturbation theory ceases to work and ОјрқңҮmu is a perturbative scale. The condition aвүӘzmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§all z ensures that the discretization effect on lattice results is small. For this special choice m0вҖӢcsubscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘җm0c, the LaMET expansion does not have a linear term in 1/Pz1superscriptрқ‘ғрқ‘§1/Pzcitebib.bib39. At one-loop order and in dimensional regularization, the renormalization factor of the logarithimic divergence iscitebib.bib8, bib.bib53, ZO=1+3вҖӢCFвҖӢОұs2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ14вҲ’d,subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯ13subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2рқңӢ14рқ‘‘displaystyle ZO=1+frac3CFalphas2pifrac14-d, where CF=4/3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№43CF=4/3 is the Casimir operator for the fundamental representation of SвҖӢUвҖӢрқ‘Ҷрқ‘Ҳ3SU and dрқ‘‘d is the space-time dimension. One can resum the logarithimic divergence through solving the renormalization group equation, dвҖӢZOвҖӢ(Пө,Ој)dвҖӢlnвҒЎ(Ој)=ОівҖӢZOвҖӢ(Пө,Ој),рқ‘‘subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯitalic-ПөрқңҮрқ‘‘рқңҮрқӣҫsubscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯitalic-ПөрқңҮdisplaystylefracdZO(epsilon,mu)dln(mu)=gamma ZO(epsilon,mu), where Пө=(4вҲ’d)/2italic-Пө4рқ‘‘2epsilon=(4-d)/2 and Оі=вҲ’3вҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢОұsвҖӢ(Ој,ОӣQCD)рқӣҫ3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқңҮsubscriptОӣQCDgamma=-frac3CF2pialphas(mu,Lambdarm QCD) is the leading anomalous dimension, which is independent of the regularization method. The form of the leading-order solution of Eq. is independent of regularization scheme and, on the lattice, is ZOвҖӢ(1/a,Ој)=(lnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD)]lnвҒЎ[Ој/ОӣQCD])3вҖӢCFb0,subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯ1рқ‘ҺрқңҮsuperscript1рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқңҮsubscriptОӣQCD3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹ0displaystyle ZO(1/a,mu)=left(fracln[1/(aLambdarm QCD)]ln[mu/Lambdarm QCD]right)frac3CFb0, where the bare ultraviolet (UV) cut-off is 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a, and the renormalization scale is ОјрқңҮmu. If one considers the contributions from sub-leading logarithms to the anomalous dimension Оірқӣҫgamma when solving the renormalization group equation Eq. , we obtaincitebib.bib54 ZOвҖӢ(1/a,Ој)=(lnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD)]lnвҒЎ[Ој/ОӣQCD])3вҖӢCFb0вҖӢ(1+dlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]),subscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘Ӯ1рқ‘ҺрқңҮsuperscript1рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқңҮsubscriptОӣQCD3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹ01рқ‘‘рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDdisplaystyle ZO(1/a,mu)=left(fracln[1/(aLambdarm QCD)]ln[mu/Lambdarm QCD]right)frac3CFb0left(1+fracdln[aLambdarm QCD]right), where ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD and the constant dрқ‘‘d depend on the specific lattice action.
content_sections.III Lattice matrix elements and simulati: The standard non-perturbative renormalization of lattice matrix elements is through calculating some auxiliary matrix elements of the operator (which can also be computed in perturbation theory) and using them as the renormalization factor to approach the continuum limit. In this paper, we focus on the study of the auxiliary matrix elements potentially useful for the renormalization of quasi-LF correlations. We mainly consider the matrix elements of the quasi-PDF operators in the following cases: 1) in a large Euclidean momentum quark state, 2) in the physical zero-momentum state of a hadron such as the pion or nucleon. We will also study the matrix element in the vacuumcitebib.bib44 (case 3), and show that it is not a good choice for renormalization because at small zрқ‘§z such a matrix element is suppressed as can be shown by operator product expansion (OPE). Moreover, since the zрқ‘§z-dependent renormalization is mainly associated with the Wilson link, we shall also consider the matrix elements of Wilson loop as well as the Wilson link in a fixed gauge (case 4). We calculate the pion and nucleon matrix elements hH0вҖӢ(z)=вҹЁH|OОіtвҖӢ(z)|Hвҹ©PвҶ’=0subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқҗ»рқ‘§subscriptquantum-operator-productрқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқҗ»вҶ’рқ‘ғ0tildeh0H(z)= H|Ogammat(z)|HvecP=0 in the rest frame evaluating the following three-point function, RHвҖӢ(t2,t,z)вүЎвҹЁOHвҖӢ(t2)вҖӢвҲ‘xвҶ’OОіtвҖӢ(z;(xвҶ’,t))вҖӢOHвҖ вҖӢвҹ©вҹЁOHвҖӢ(t2)вҖӢOHвҖ вҖӢвҹ©subscriptрқ‘…рқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўрқ‘§delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ў2subscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§вҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗ»вҖ 0delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ў2superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗ»вҖ 0displaystyle RH(t2,t,z)equivfrac OH(t2)sumvecxOgammat(z;(vecx,t))OHdagger OH(t2)OHdagger =вҹЁH|OОіtвҖӢ(z)|Hвҹ©absentquantum-operator-productрқҗ»subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқҗ»displaystyle= H|Ogammat(z)|H +рқ’ӘвҖӢ(eвҲ’ОҙвҖӢmвҖӢt)+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(eвҲ’ОҙвҖӢmвҖӢ(t2вҲ’t))+рқ’ӘвҖӢ(eвҲ’ОҙвҖӢmвҖӢt2),рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝрқ‘ҡрқ‘Ўрқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўрқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝрқ‘ҡsubscriptрқ‘Ў2displaystyle+cal O(e-delta mt)+cal O(e-delta m(t2-t))+cal O(e-delta mt2), where OОіtвҖӢ(z,(xвҶ’,t))=ПҲВҜвҖӢ(xвҶ’,t)вҖӢОіtвҖӢUвҖӢ((xвҶ’,t),(xвҶ’+nzвҖӢz,t))вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(xвҶ’+nzвҖӢz,t)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§вҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎВҜрқң“вҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҲвҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқ‘§рқ‘Ўрқң“вҶ’рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§рқ‘§рқ‘ЎOgammat(z,(vecx,t))=barpsi(vecx,t)gammatU((vecx,t),(vecx+hatnzz,t))psi(vecx+hatnzz,t), nzsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘§hatnz is the unitary vector along the zрқ‘§z direction, and OHsubscriptрқ‘Ӯрқҗ»OH is the interpolation field of a hadron such as a pion and a nucleon. t2subscriptрқ‘Ў2t2 corresponds to the source-sink separation. To obtain hH0вҖӢ(z)subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқҗ»рқ‘§tildeh0H(z) accurately, we need both tрқ‘Ўt and t2вҲ’tsubscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўt2-t to be large enough to suppress excited-state contaminations, or fit the three point function with a proper parametrization on the excited-state contaminations. We can use the first strategy for the pion case since the signal-to-noise ratio will not decay for the pion in the rest frame; while the second strategy is essential for the other cases where the statistical uncertainty increases exponentially with tрқ‘Ўt and t2subscriptрқ‘Ў2t2. Due to (anti)periodic boundary conditions we can use at most t2=T/2subscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ү2t2=T/2 which is larger than 2 fm on most modern lattice ensembles. Since the mass gap ОҙвҖӢmвҲјsimilar-toрқӣҝрқ‘ҡabsentdelta msim 1 GeV between the ground and first exited state of pion вҹЁhПҖ0вҖӢ(z)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқңӢрқ‘§tildeh0pi(z) can be extracted with sufficient accuracy. At small perturbative zрқ‘§z, вҹЁhПҖ0вҖӢ(z)вҹ©вҲ’1superscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқңӢрқ‘§1tildeh0pi(z)-1 acts as a renormalization factor up to certain рқ’ӘвҖӢ(ОӣQCD2вҖӢz2)рқ’ӘsuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2superscriptрқ‘§2cal O(Lambdarm QCD2z2) power correctionscitebib.bib41. A common non-perturbative renormalization method for lattice QCD matrix elements with local operators is the RI/MOM schemecitebib.bib55 and its modified versionscitebib.bib56. One can calculate the bare matrix element with given operators in an off-shell quark state, for both the lattice and dimensional regularizations, and renormalize their difference in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinetextrmMS scheme to get the renormalization factor for the bare quantities. For the quasi-PDF, such a renormalization constant (the RI/MOM renormalization factor) is defined through the OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix element in the off-shell quark state with given momentum p2superscriptрқ‘қ2p2citebib.bib38, bib.bib40, bib.bib34, bib.bib57: ZRвҖӢIвҖӢ(z,ОјR)=14вҖӢNcвҖӢTrвҖӢ[ОіtвҖӢвҹЁq|OОіtвҖӢ(z)|qвҹ©]|p2=вҲ’ОјR2,pz=pt=0,superscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқҗјрқ‘§subscriptрқңҮрқ‘…evaluated-at14subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җTrdelimited-subscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўquantum-operator-productрқ‘һsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқ‘һformulae-sequencesuperscriptрқ‘қ2superscriptsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘…2subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘Ў0displaystyle ZRI(z,muR)=frac14NctextrmTr[gammat q|Ogammat(z)|q]|p2=-muR2,pz=pt=0, where Nc=3subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җ3Nc=3 is the number of colors, ОјRsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘…muR is the RI/MOM renormalization scale. We will take ОјRsubscriptрқңҮрқ‘…muR = 3 GeV since the RI/MOM factor has little dependence on the renormalizaiton scale in a certain rangecitebib.bib45. We work with Landau gauge fixed configurations, and choose pz=pt=0subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘Ў0pz=pt=0 to eliminate the difference between the projectionscitebib.bib16. Factor ZRвҖӢIsuperscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқҗјZRI can be calculated non-perturbatively for any lattice regularization, or equivalently for any quark and gluon action defined on the lattice. However, the current lattice QCD calculation is limited to the Landau gauge. The perturbative matching with off-shell states in Landau gauge can be complicated beyond one-loop levelcitebib.bib58. The zрқ‘§z-dependent part of renormalization comes from the Wilson line, therefore one may extract the linear divergences from matrix elements of pure Wilson lines. First one can consider a Wilson loopcitebib.bib43, bib.bib27, bib.bib59, bib.bib38, bib.bib28, рқ’°вҖӢ(r,t)=вҹЁUвҖӢ(rвҶ’,t;rвҶ’,0)вҖӢUвҖӢ(rвҶ’,0;0вҶ’,0)вҖӢUвҖӢ(0вҶ’,0;0вҶ’,t)вҖӢUвҖӢ(0вҶ’,t;rвҶ’,t)вҹ©,рқ’°рқ‘ҹрқ‘Ўdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘ҲвҶ’рқ‘ҹрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ҹ0рқ‘ҲвҶ’рқ‘ҹ0вҶ’00рқ‘ҲвҶ’00вҶ’0рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҲвҶ’0рқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘ҹрқ‘Ўcal U(r,t)= U(vecr,t;vecr,0)U(vecr,0;vec0,0)U(vec0,0;vec0,t)U(vec0,t;vecr,t), Ref. citebib.bib43 proposed this scheme to renormalize the pion DA, and the linear divergence coefficient was extracted precisely based on the calculation with several lattice spacings in the following Kaon DA studycitebib.bib27. But most of subsequent lattice QCD calculations have switched to the RI/MOM scheme or used a hadronic matrix element. One can also consider the matrix element of a single Wilson Link вҹЁUвҖӢ(0,z)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҳ0рқ‘§ U(0,z) in a fixed gauge such as the Landau gauge. As the simplest choice without any external state, вҹЁUвҖӢ(0,z)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқ‘Ҳ0рқ‘§ U(0,z) can provide a reference to identify whether the linearly divergent behavior is sensitive to the existence of the external state, as suggested in the multiplicative renormalizability studies of the quasi-PDF operatorcitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38. Our lattice calculations are performed using the Chroma software suitecitebib.bib60 and QUDAcitebib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63 in the HIP programming modelcitebib.bib64. We use 2+1+1 flavors (degenerate up and down, strange, and charm degrees of freedom) of highly improved staggered quarks (HISQ)citebib.bib65 ensembles generated by the MILC Collaborationcitebib.bib46 at five lattice spacings, and 2+1 flavor domain wall (DW) quarks and Iwasaki gauge ensembles from the RBC/UKQCD collaborationcitebib.bib47 at three lattice spacings. The pion mass for all ensembles are tuned to be roughly 310 MeV based on the pion mass of the light sea quark mass on the corresponding ensemble. The lattice spacings for the MILC ensembles are determined using Wilson flow based on the parameters determined by Ref. citebib.bib66. We use the matrix elements without hyper-cubic (HYP) smearing in order to test the perturbative renormalization analysis (Sec. ,, and). However, since in practical calculations the results without smearing can be rather noisy, it is standard to use some type of smearing in lattice simulations. It is unclear to us how strongly the smearing will interfere with renormalization. Therefore, we regard our method as a purely phenomenological approach for the time being to analyze data with one step HYP smearingcitebib.bib67 in Sec. and, hoping that the gain in statistical precision outweighs the additional systematic uncertainty from moderate smearing. We use two kinds of fermion actions for the valence quarks: clover and overlap fermions. Clover fermions break chiral symmetry and the action is defined by SqcвҖӢlвҖӢv=subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘җрқ‘ҷрқ‘Јрқ‘һabsentdisplaystyle Sclvq= вҲ‘xПҲВҜ(x)[1вҲ’ОіОј2вҖӢaUОј(x,x+nОј)ПҲ(x+nОј)displaystylesumxbarpsi(x)bigg[frac1-gammamu2aUmu(x,x+hatnmu)psi(x+hatnmu) +1+ОіОј2вҖӢaвҖӢUОјвҖӢ(x,xвҲ’nОј)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(xвҲ’nОј)1subscriptрқӣҫрқңҮ2рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘ҲрқңҮрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮрқң“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮdisplaystyle+frac1+gammamu2aUmu(x,x-hatnmu)psi(x-hatnmu) вҲ’(4a+csвҖӢwПғОјвҖӢОҪFОјвҖӢОҪ(x)a+mq0)ПҲ(x)],displaystyle-left(frac4a+cswsigmamunuFmunu(x)a+mq0right)psi(x)bigg], where nОјsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮhatnmu is the unit vector along the ОјрқңҮmu direction, the clover coefficient csвҖӢwsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘Өcsw is the tadpole improved tree level value, and mq0вҖӢasubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘һрқ‘Һm0qa is the bare quark mass which is determined by requiring the pion mass to be roughly 310 MeV. Parameters csвҖӢwsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘ рқ‘Өcsw and mq0вҖӢasubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘һрқ‘Һm0qa should approach 1 and 0 respectively in the continuum, while the lattice spacing dependence is weaker than the рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a)рқ’Әрқ‘Һcal O(a) discretization effect in the present range of aрқ‘Һa, and closer to that of the gauge coupling g2superscriptрқ‘”2g2 as predicted by lattice perturbative theory. The overlap action preserves chiral symmetry but the simulation is rather expensive. We use it to test the dependence of renormalization on the fermion action. The overlap fermion action is defined bycitebib.bib68, bib.bib69 Sqovsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Ҷovрқ‘һdisplaystyle Stextrmovq =вҲ‘x,yПҲВҜвҖӢ(x)вҖӢDovвҖӢ(x,y)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(y),absentsubscriptрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰВҜрқң“рқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқҗ·ovрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұрқң“рқ‘Ұdisplaystyle=sumx,ybarpsi(x)Dtextrmov(x,y)psi(y), Dovsubscriptрқҗ·ovdisplaystyle Dtextrmov =ПҒвҖӢ(1+DwвҖӢ(вҲ’ПҒ)DwвҖ вҖӢ(вҲ’ПҒ)вҖӢDwвҖӢ(вҲ’ПҒ)),absentрқңҢ1subscriptрқҗ·wрқңҢsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗ·вҖ wрқңҢsubscriptрқҗ·wрқңҢdisplaystyle=rhoBig(1+fracDtextrmw(-rho)sqrtDdaggertextrmw(-rho)Dtextrmw(-rho)Big), where DwвҖӢ(mqw;x,y)=1вҲ’ОіОј2вҖӢaвҖӢUвҖӢ(x,x+nОј)вҖӢОҙx+nОј,ysubscriptрқҗ·wsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҡwрқ‘һрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұ1subscriptрқӣҫрқңҮ2рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮрқ‘Ұdisplaystyle Dtextrmw(mtextrmwq;x,y)=frac1-gammamu2aU(x,x+hatnmu)deltax+hatnmu,y +1+ОіОј2вҖӢaвҖӢUвҖӢ(x,xвҲ’nОј)вҖӢОҙxвҲ’nОј,yвҲ’(4a+mqw)вҖӢОҙx,y,1subscriptрқӣҫрқңҮ2рқ‘Һрқ‘Ҳрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘Ҙsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқңҮрқ‘Ұ4рқ‘Һsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘ҡwрқ‘һsubscriptрқӣҝрқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұdisplaystyle+frac1+gammamu2aU(x,x-hatnmu)deltax-hatnmu,y-(frac4a+mtextrmwq)deltax,y, and вҲ’ПҒрқңҢ-rho should be smaller than the bare quark mass for which vanishes the pion mass to make Dovsubscriptрқҗ·ovDtextrmov to be the same as the standard Dirac operator in the continuum limit. We choose вҲ’ПҒ=1.5рқңҢ1.5-rho=1.5. Although different active and sea fermion formulations will in general introduce non-unitarity, the effect shall vanish in the continuum limit. However, at finite aрқ‘Һa, the difference will generate systematic uncertainties which can affect the final results. We start with nucleon and pion matrix elements for the MILC ensembles and clover action. For the pion matrix element, We average the RПҖвҖӢ(t2,t,z)subscriptрқ‘…рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўрқ‘§Rpi(t2,t,z) data with t2=T/2subscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ү2t2=T/2 and tвҲҲ[T/8,3вҖӢT/8]рқ‘Ўрқ‘Ү83рқ‘Ү8tin[T/8,3T/8] to get a precise estimate of the ground state matrix element, and it should be also precise since Tрқ‘ҮT is between 7.7 and 9.1 fm in the ensembles we used, as shown in the upper panel of Fig. . Note that RПҖвҖӢ(T/2,T/4,z)subscriptрқ‘…рқңӢрқ‘Ү2рқ‘Ү4рқ‘§Rpi(T/2,T/4,z) equals to вҹЁПҖ|OОіtвҖӢ(z)|ПҖвҹ©/2quantum-operator-productрқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§рқңӢ2pi|Ogammat(z)|pi/2 in such a case. The additional factor 1/2 corrects for the fact that there are forward and backward propagating states for (anti)periodic boundary conditions. The nucleon case is known to be much noisier especially at large t2subscriptрқ‘Ў2t2. Thus, we just consider t2=2вҖӢtвүғ0.72subscriptрқ‘Ў22рқ‘Ўsimilar-to-or-equals0.72t2=2tsimeq 0.72 fm in all cases, and plot RNвҖӢ(t2,t,z)subscriptрқ‘…рқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўрқ‘§RN(t2,t,z) in the lower panel of Fig. . The normalized bare hN0вҖӢ(z)/hN0вҖӢsubscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғ0tildeh0N(z)/tildeh0N where hN0вҖӢ(z)subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғрқ‘§tildeh0N(z) is approximated by RNвҖӢ(t2,t,z)subscriptрқ‘…рқ‘Ғsubscriptрқ‘Ў2рқ‘Ўрқ‘§RN(t2,t,z) with t2=2вҖӢtвүғ0.72subscriptрқ‘Ў22рқ‘Ўsimilar-to-or-equals0.72t2=2tsimeq 0.72 fm is plotted in Fig. , with a normalization factor 1/hN0вҖӢ1subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғ01/tildeh0N using jackknife resampling to make it to be exactly one at z=0рқ‘§0z=0 at all lattice spacings. As in the figure, the linear divergence makes hN0вҖӢ(z)/hN0вҖӢsubscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғрқ‘§subscriptsuperscriptв„Һ0рқ‘Ғ0tildeh0N(z)/tildeh0N decay exponentially with both zрқ‘§z and aрқ‘Һa, and thus one cannot obtain any meaningful continuum limit when aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0ato 0. A renormalization is necessary to recover a good continuum limit.
content_sections.IV Simple Test of Linear Divergence: To study the renormalization properties of the quasi-PDF operator, we need to calculate matrix elements at several different lattice spacings. To ensure the data to be useful for a refined high-precision analysis, we start by testing whether they approximately show the linear divergence predicted by perturbation theory. In particular, one needs to show that the zрқ‘§z-dependence of the linearly divergent term is linear. To achieve this, we first extract the factor eвҲ’ОҙвҖӢmВҜвҖӢzsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡрқ‘§e-deltabarmz from the bare matrix element to test the 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a dependence in ОҙвҖӢmВҜрқӣҝВҜрқ‘ҡdeltabarm in Eq. . Based on Eqs. ,, and, if we take the natural log on a bare matrix element в„ів„іM, we have, lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=eвҖӢ(z)aвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]+gвҖӢ(z),в„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘’рқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқ‘”рқ‘§displaystylelnM(z,a)=frace(z)aln[aLambdarm QCD]+g(z), where the first term is linearly divergent and gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) is the residual. We have ignored the logarithmic divergence and discretization error here because these terms are much smaller and thus have little influence on this simple test of the linear divergence. We use Eq. to fit the bare matrix elements as a function of aрқ‘Һa. We treat eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z) and gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) as unknown functions of zрқ‘§z so that our fitting is performed at each value of zрқ‘§z at which they are treated as free parameters. We need to do linear interpolation on the data lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)в„ірқ‘§рқ‘ҺlnM(z,a) with respect to zрқ‘§z. Here are the steps we perform in detail: 1. We do the linear interpolation with neighbourhood two data points to obtain the central value and uncertainty at z=0.06Г—nрқ‘§0.06рқ‘ӣz=0.06times n fm (n=3,4,5,вҖҰ,16)рқ‘ӣ345вҖҰ16(n=3,4,5,. . . ,16). 2. For each zрқ‘§z, we fit the dependence on aрқ‘Һa, treating eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z), gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) as fitted parameters. For this initial test, we fix ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD at 0.2 GeV, which is a reasonable guess to start withcitebib.bib70. 3. We plot the fitted eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z) with respect to zрқ‘§z to see if eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z) has a linear zрқ‘§z dependence. If we obtain a reasonable fit in step 2, we then see approximately the 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a dependence of the divergences. In step 3, we can test the linear zрқ‘§z dependence. The fitting results for the bare OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix element in the off-shell quark state for valence overlap and clover actions without HYP smearing are shown in Fig. and Fig. , respectively. There are eight different lattice spacings. The range of zрқ‘§z is taken from 0.18fm to 0.96fm and we analyse 14 different zрқ‘§z values in this range. The 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a dependence in both cases is fitted very well, although due to the high precision of the data, chi-square is large. However, this is not our concern at this initial step. The zрқ‘§z dependence of the divergent coefficients shows nicely the linear feature, approximately going through zero at z=0рқ‘§0z=0. This is a strong indication that the linear divergence follows roughly the prediction of perturbation theory which gives us confidence to perform more refined analyses in the next sections.
content_sections.V Self-Renormalization of lattice matrix: The non-perturbative matrix elements contain the following important contributions: a) the linear divergence, b) a finite term coming from non-perturbative renormalon physics, and c) the residual contribution encoding intrinsic non-perturbative physics. It is part c) that is required to extract the partonic structure information. Thus, it is important to separate out the latter from the former in a systematic way and with high precision. Here we develop a self-renormalization method to extract the residual intrinsic non-perturbative physics and renormalization factor from a matrix element itself without using a different matrix element. The extraction of the residual is much harder than the extraction of the linear divergence term because after we take the logarithm of the matrix element (Eq. ), the linear divergence term is dominant and the residual is very sensitive to the subtraction. We need to properly take into account the fine features of the data, including logarithmic divergences and discretization errors. Based on Eq. , we modify the fitting function Eq. to be, lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=kвҖӢzaвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]+gвҖӢ(z)+f1,2вҖӢ(z)вҖӢaв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘“12рқ‘§рқ‘ҺdisplaystylelnM(z,a)=frackzaln[aLambdarm QCD]+g(z)+f1,2(z)a +3вҖӢCFb0вҖӢlnвҒЎ[lnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD)]lnвҒЎ[Ој/ОӣQCD]]+lnвҒЎ[1+dlnвҒЎ(aвҖӢОӣQCD)],3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹ01рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқңҮsubscriptОӣQCD1рқ‘‘рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDdisplaystyle+frac3CFb0lnbigg[fracln[1/(aLambdarm QCD)]ln[mu/Lambdarm QCD]bigg]+lnbigg[1+fracdln(aLambdarm QCD)bigg], where the first term is linearly divergent. gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) is the residual, which contains the non-perturbative m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 effect and the intrinsic non-perturbative physics. f1,2вҖӢ(z)вҖӢasubscriptрқ‘“12рқ‘§рқ‘Һf1,2(z)a takes into account the discretization errors, which we allow to be different for the data calculated from MILC and RBC ensembles (f1вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“1рқ‘§f1(z) for MILC and f2вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘§f2(z) for RBC). The last two terms come from the resummation of leading and sub-leading logarithmic divergences, which only affect the overall normalization at different lattice spacings. We use Eq. to fit the logarithms to the data for the bare matrix elements for each zрқ‘§z. The zрқ‘§z points can be chosen in a large range where the lattice discretization error is small and, at the same time, the statistical error is limited. We fit the dependence on aрқ‘Һa, treating gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z), f1вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“1рқ‘§f1(z), f2вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘§f2(z) as free parameters and fixing ОјрқңҮmu at 2 GeV, which is the renormalization scale we choose to use. We will discuss kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD in detail in Sec. and dрқ‘‘d in Sec. . We obtain the residual gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) from the fit. The gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) obtained this way does not correspond to what is in the perturbative MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme because it contains a non-perturbative m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 effect. We need to eliminate this effect through matching the lattice result to the continuum scheme at short distance zрқ‘§z. Based on Eq. and Eq. , we use the following equation within a window aвүӘz<1/Ојmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§1рқңҮall z<1/mu to extract m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0, gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’lnвҒЎ[ZMSВҜвҖӢ(z,Ој,ОӣMSВҜ)]=m0вҖӢz,рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSрқ‘§рқңҮsubscriptОӣВҜMSsubscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§displaystyle g(z)-ln[ZoverlineMS(z,mu,LambdaoverlineMS)]=m0z, where ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS is the perturbative matrix element in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlineMS scheme. For the OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix elements in the pion, nucleon, and off-shell quark state, we take ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS at one loopcitebib.bib42, ZMSВҜвҖӢ(z,Ој,ОӣMSВҜ)=1+ОұsвҖӢ(Ој,ОӣMSВҜ)вҖӢCF2вҖӢПҖвҖӢ[32вҖӢlnвҒЎz2вҖӢОј24вҖӢeвҲ’2вҖӢОіE+52],subscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSрқ‘§рқңҮsubscriptОӣВҜMS1subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқңҮsubscriptОӣВҜMSsubscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№2рқңӢdelimited-32superscriptрқ‘§2superscriptрқңҮ24superscriptрқ‘’2subscriptрқӣҫрқҗё52displaystyle ZoverlineMS(z,mu,LambdaoverlineMS)=1+fracalphas(mu,LambdaoverlineMS)CF2pibigg[frac32lnfracz2mu24e-2gammaE+frac52bigg] , from operator product expansion, where we fix ОјрқңҮmu = 2 GeV and ОӣMSВҜsubscriptОӣВҜMSLambdaoverlineMS = 0.3 GeV. The renormalized physical matrix element is then obtained from в„івҖӢ(z)R=expвҒЎ[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢz],в„іsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘…рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§displaystyleM(z)R=exp[g(z)-m0z], and is expected to be consistent with ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS at small zрқ‘§z. One could consider higher-order MSВҜВҜMSoverlineMS matrix elements for matching, including summing over large logarithms. We choose not to do this here as it does not affect the procedure for the subsequent analysis, and therefore does not change the main conclusions of the paper. However, to obtain physical results at high precisions, one might need to examine this more carefully. We thus define a renormalization factor Z(z,a)R=exp[kвҖӢzaвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]+m0z+f1,2(z)adisplaystyle Z(z,a)R=expBig[frackzaln[aLambdarm QCD]+m0z+f1,2(z)a +3вҖӢCFb0ln[lnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD)]lnвҒЎ[Ој/ОӣQCD]]+ln[1+dlnвҒЎ(aвҖӢОӣQCD)]],displaystyle+frac3CFb0lnbigg[fracln[1/(aLambdarm QCD)]ln[mu/Lambdarm QCD]bigg]+ln[1+fracdln(aLambdarm QCD)]Big] , which includes the discretization error as well. All the parameters here are either fixed, fitted or fine-tuned through the renormalization procedure from the matrix element в„івҖӢ(z,a)в„ірқ‘§рқ‘ҺM(z,a) itself. Dividing the bare matrix element в„івҖӢ(z,a)в„ірқ‘§рқ‘ҺM(z,a) by ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…Z(z,a)R, we get в„іRвҖӢ(z)=в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)R. subscriptв„ірқ‘…рқ‘§в„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…displaystylewidetildeMR(z)=M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R. If our procedure eliminates all the divergences and discretization errors, we should expect that в„іRвҖӢ(z)subscriptв„ірқ‘…рқ‘§widetildeMR(z) is aрқ‘Һa-independent and the same as в„івҖӢ(z)Rв„іsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘…M(z)R (Eq. ). The degree to which this is the case can be used as a test of the self-renormalization procedure.

Now let us turn to the parameter kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD in Eq. . In Eq. , we have only considered the leading order perturbative term for the linear divergence and neglected higher-order ones. These can be included partially by a proper choice of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. For example, the coupling constants with different choices of ОӣОӣLambda are related to each other by perturbation theorycitebib.bib70, ОұsвҖӢ(Q,ОӣQCD)вҲјОұsвҖӢ(Q,Оӣ)+c2вҖӢОұs2вҖӢ(Q,Оӣ)similar-tosubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘„subscriptОӣQCDsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘„Оӣsubscriptрқ‘җ2superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 2рқ‘„Оӣdisplaystylealphas(Q,Lambdarm QCD)simalphas(Q,Lambda)+c2alphas2(Q,Lambda) +c3вҖӢОұs3вҖӢ(Q,Оӣ)+вҖҰsubscriptрқ‘җ3superscriptsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 3рқ‘„ОӣвҖҰdisplaystyle+c3alphas3(Q,Lambda)+. . . In principle, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD depends on the specific lattice action, and one needs to perform multi-loop calculations to relate them. In our analysis, we treat ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD as a fitting parameter which, therefore, can effectively take into account some higher-order effects. Next we want to discuss the coefficient kрқ‘ҳk of the linear divergence. QCD perturbation theory not only predicts the 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a dependence and linear zрқ‘§z dependence (which we have tested in Sec. ), but also the value of kрқ‘ҳk. Comparing Eq. and Eq. , we get the one-loop perturbative value of kрқ‘ҳk: k=(2вҖӢПҖ)23вҖӢb0=1.46=7.4вҖӢGeVвҲ’1вҖӢfmвҲ’1. рқ‘ҳsuperscript2рқңӢ23subscriptрқ‘Ҹ01.467.4superscriptGeV1superscriptfm1displaystyle k=frac(2pi)23b0=1.46=7.4rm GeV-1fm-1. Next, we check whether this value is consistent with our data. We use Eq. to fit the bare OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix element in the pion state for the clover action without HYP smearing, treating gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z), f1вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“1рқ‘§f1(z), f2вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘§f2(z) as fitted parameters. If we take (kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD) to be one set of values, e. g. , (7.4 GeVfm, 0.09 GeV), we can perform a fitting for each zрқ‘§z, as seen in Fig. . There is a ПҮ2/d. o. f. formulae-sequencesuperscriptрқң’2dofchi2/rm d. o. f. for the fit at each zрқ‘§z and we calculate the average of them, denoted as вҹЁПҮ2/d. o. f. вҹ©zchi2/rm d. o. f. z. Varying (kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD) generates a ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 map, as seen in Fig. . The sets of (kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD) of small ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 lie in a band, which we call the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ. That means that kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD are strongly correlated, while there is a large uncertainty for kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD separately. If we choose some sets of values from the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ to do the fitting, we find the fitted residuals Exp[gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z)] are very different, as seen in Fig. . However, after eliminating the non-perturbative m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 effect, the renormalized matrix elements Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] are all the same (see Fig. ). Therefore, the uncertainty in kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD will not significantly influence the final physical result. The reason for this uncertainty lies in the behavior of kaвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDfrackaln[aLambdarm QCD] (the term related to the linear divergence in Eq. ) in the aрқ‘Һa range of our data. Varying along the вҖқsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ effectively shifts kaвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]рқ‘ҳрқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDfrackaln[aLambdarm QCD] by a constant Cрқҗ¶C, see Fig. . The CвҖӢzрқҗ¶рқ‘§Cz term is absorbed into gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) automatically during fitting so there is a large uncertainty for kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. However, when we eliminate the m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 effect, the CвҖӢzрқҗ¶рқ‘§Cz term has been taken into account, and the final result does not change. Another way to interpret this is that the perturbative value of kрқ‘ҳk is consistent with the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2вҖқ band and therefore is consistent with the fit. As a consequence, we can fix kрқ‘ҳk at the one-loop perturbative value, 7.4 GeVвҲ’1вҖӢfmвҲ’1superscriptGeV1superscriptfm1rm GeV-1fm-1 (or 1.46 if dimensionless) in the subsequent analysis. Even with this choice of kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD can still vary within a reasonable range of ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 while the physical result is stabilized by the choice of m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0. We call this correlation between ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD and m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 the вҖңphenomenological renormalon uncertaintyвҖқ, in the sense that ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD is characteristic for the different truncation/resummation schemes for the perturbation series, but that the non-perturbative m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 compensates the effects of the differencescitebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52.

In principle, the parameter dрқ‘‘d in Eq. is determined by the lattice action. In our data, there are two different dynamical lattice ensembles (MILC and RBC) and two different valence fermion actions (overlap and clover). Here we treat dрқ‘‘d as a fine-tuning parameter to make sure that (gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’lnвҒЎ[ZMSВҜвҖӢ(z,Ој,ОӣMSВҜ)])рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSрқ‘§рқңҮsubscriptОӣВҜMS(g(z)-ln[ZoverlineMS(z,mu,LambdaoverlineMS)]) in Eq. is proportional to zрқ‘§z within a window aвүӘz<1/Ојmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§1рқңҮall z<1/mu. As shown in Fig. , this is quite effective. We have also tested that dрқ‘‘d has little influence on ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 since the effect of tuning dрқ‘‘d shifts gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) simply by a constant. Fig. shows that the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] (blue) for clover quarks is consistent with ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS (black) at small zрқ‘§z. However, at large zрқ‘§z, there is a significant discrepancy between Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] and ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS. That means that there is a significant non-perturbative effect at large zрқ‘§z in the popular RI/MOM and ratio renormalization schemes used previously. To our knowledge, this is the first time that this difference has been studied in the literature. This finding supports the usage of the hybrid renormalization procedure proposed recentlycitebib.bib39.

The procedure developed in the previous subsections forms our self-renormalization strategy. This procedure can overcome some of the problems encountered in the renormalization of the linear divergence due to the numerical uncertainties associated with exponentially-amplified small errors. There are three steps in this process: 1. Use Eq. to fit the data of the bare matrix elements в„ів„іM. For each zрқ‘§z (here zрқ‘§z can be chosen in a large range and linear interpolation with respect to zрқ‘§z might be needed for the data in lnв„івҖӢ(z,a)в„ірқ‘§рқ‘ҺM(z,a) ), fit the dependence on aрқ‘Һa, treating ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD as a global parameter, namely the same for all zрқ‘§z, and gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z), f1вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“1рқ‘§f1(z), f2вҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘“2рқ‘§f2(z) as fit parameters, with fixing kрқ‘ҳk=7.4 GeVfm (or 1.46 if dimensionless), and ОјрқңҮmu=2 GeV. Fine-tune dрқ‘‘d to make sure that gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’lnвҒЎ[ZMSВҜ]рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSg(z)-ln[ZoverlineMS] is proportional to zрқ‘§z within a window aвүӘz<1/Ојmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§1рқңҮall z<1/mu. We obtain the residual gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) through fitting; 2. Use Eq. to fit the dependence on zрқ‘§z (within a window aвүӘz<1/Ојmuch-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§1рқңҮall z<1/mu) to extract m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0. Then calculate the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] (Eq. ), which is considered valid for a large range of zрқ‘§z; 3. Calculate в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R (Eq. ) to see if showing any dependence on the lattice constant aрқ‘Һa and compare with Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z]. In step 1 and 2, one can use another way to get dрқ‘‘d and m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0: Modify Eq. through replacing gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z) with (lnвҒЎ[ZMSВҜвҖӢ(z,Ој,ОӣMSВҜ)]+m0вҖӢzsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSрқ‘§рқңҮsubscriptОӣВҜMSsubscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§ln[ZoverlineMS(z,mu,LambdaoverlineMS)]+m0z), and then use the modified fitting function to fit the bare matrix element at small zрқ‘§z to extract dрқ‘‘d and m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0. This way should be equivalent to the procedure outlined above and we will not follow it here. In step 3, we need to calculate ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…Z(z,a)R as well as estimate its error. We shall not propagate the fitting error of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD to ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…Z(z,a)R because the effect of slight variation of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD can be compensated by m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 and will not influence our result as we have discussed in Sec. . Since the lattice data we use do not provide systematic errors, we will add a dummy systematic error Оҙsyssubscriptрқӣҝsysdeltarm sys to the data during our fittingcitebib.bib45 (ПғlnвҒЎв„і)new=(ПғlnвҒЎв„і)old2+(ОҙsysвҖӢaвҖӢОј)2,subscriptsubscriptрқңҺв„іnewsuperscriptsubscriptsubscriptрқңҺв„іold2superscriptsubscriptрқӣҝsysрқ‘ҺрқңҮ2displaystyle(sigmalnM)rm new=sqrt(sigmalnM)rm old2+(deltarm sysamu)2, where we assume that there is an aвҖӢОјрқ‘ҺрқңҮamu dependence as well. This choice is intended to give more weight to the small lattice spacing data in the fitting.

In this subsection, we apply the self-renormalization method to the OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) matrix elements in the pion, nucleon, and off-shell quark state with valence clover and overlap actions without HYP smearing. We compare the renormalization factors and physics results. We find that apart from the case of the RI/MOM matrix element with clover valence fermion, all other renormalization factors are similar. Despite this difference in renormalization factor, the physical results are independent of fermion actions. Figs. through show the detailed results of applying the self-renormalization method for different cases. For the RI/MOM factor with overlap and clover fermions, we analyse eight different lattice spacings from two different types of gauge ensembles. For the pion matrix element calculated with the overlap fermion action, we analyse three different lattice spacings from MILC ensembles. In the clover pion case, we use six different lattice spacings from two different types of gauge ensembles. For the nucleon matrix element with clover fermion action, we analyse five different lattice spacings from MILC ensembles. Subfigure (a) in each figure is used to estimate the best fitted value of the global parameter ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD as well as its error. For each chosen ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD, we can use Eq. to fit the bare matrix element. The fitting for each zрқ‘§z gives us a separate ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2. We can calculate the averaged ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 for different zрқ‘§z, denoted as вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©zsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§chi2z. Subfigure (a) shows вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©zвҲ’[вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©z]minsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§subscriptdelimited-subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§minchi2z-[chi2z]rm min with respect to ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©zвҲ’[вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©z]minsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§subscriptdelimited-subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§minchi2z-[chi2z]rm min=0 gives us the best fitted ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD and вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©zвҲ’[вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©z]minsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§subscriptdelimited-subscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§minchi2z-[chi2z]rm min=1 gives us its error. If the data points for different zрқ‘§z were independent, we should use the total ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 for the fits at different zрқ‘§z to estimate the error of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. However, since we have done linear interpolations of the original data points, some of the points are correlated to each other. So here we just use вҹЁПҮ2вҹ©zsubscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©superscriptрқң’2рқ‘§chi2z to estimate the error of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. The range of zрқ‘§z starts from 0.18 fm here but not 0.06 fm because the ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 for zрқ‘§z = 0.06 or 0.12 fm is much larger than others. dрқ‘‘d is fixed to be вҲ’11-1 since it has little influence on ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2. For Оҙsys=0.002subscriptрқӣҝsys0.002deltarm sys=0.002, the best fitted values of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD are 0.1086, 0.1350, 0.093, 0.086, 0.0926 GeV for the correlations in the overlap quark, clover quark, overlap pion, clover pion, and clover nucleon, respectively. As one can see, they are quite similar except for the correlation in the clover quark state. While the size of the systematic error has some effects for the correlation in the quark case, it has little influence on the correlation in the physical states. In subfigure (b) in each figure, after taking ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD to be the best fitted value, we use Eq. to fit the bare matrix elements to extract gвҖӢ(z)рқ‘”рқ‘§g(z). The range of zрқ‘§z is taken from 0.06 fm to 1.02 fm with 17 different zрқ‘§z values for correlations in the overlap quark case, from 0.06 fm to 1.14 fm, with 19 different zрқ‘§z values for correlations in the overlap pion and clover pion cases, from 0.06 fm to 0.96 fm, with 16 different zрқ‘§z in the clover quark case, from 0.06 fm to 0.9 fm, and with 15 different zрқ‘§z values in the clover nucleon case. We fine-tune dрқ‘‘d to make sure that gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’lnвҒЎ[ZMSВҜ]рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSg(z)-ln[ZoverlineMS] is proportional to zрқ‘§z within the window 0.06 fm вүӨzвүӨabsentрқ‘§absentleq zleq 0.24 fm. The fine-tuned dрқ‘‘d values are вҲ’1.29,вҲ’1.35,вҲ’1.17,вҲ’0.92,вҲ’0.971.291.351.170.920.97-1.29,-1.35,-1.17,-0.92,-0.97 in the five cases, respectively. They are all close to вҲ’11-1. Subfigure (c) in each figure shows the fitting to extract m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0. For Оҙsys=0.002subscriptрқӣҝsys0.002deltarm sys=0.002, the fitted values of m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 are 0.2339, 0.7667, вҲ’-0.181, вҲ’0.363вҖӢ0.36325-0.363, and вҲ’0.257вҖӢ0.25720-0.257 fm in the five cases, respectively. They are all about the order of ОӣQCDвҲј0.1similar-tosubscriptОӣQCD0.1Lambdarm QCDsim 0.1 GeV (about 0.5 fm), which supports the argument in Sec. that m0subscriptрқ‘ҡ0m0 originates from the non-perturbative renormalon effect. In subfigure (d) in each figure, we show our result for the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] in blue points connected by lines. As zрқ‘§z increases, Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] increases first, following the perturbative result, and then decreases from about zрқ‘§z = 0.25 fm on. In all the cases, Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] is consistent with ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS at small zрқ‘§z. However, at large zрқ‘§z, there is a significant discrepancy between Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] and ZMSВҜsubscriptрқ‘ҚВҜMSZoverlineMS. This means that there is a large non-perturbative effect at large zрқ‘§z in the popular RI/MOM and ratio renormalization scheme used previously, which supports the hybrid renormalization procedure proposed recentlycitebib.bib39. We have already briefly discussed this in Sec. . Subfigure (e) shows the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] for different Оҙsyssubscriptрқӣҝsysdeltarm sys. A change in Оҙsyssubscriptрқӣҝsysdeltarm sys does not influence the central values of Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] for most cases except for the clover pion. In the clover pion case, the increase of Оҙsyssubscriptрқӣҝsysdeltarm sys can slightly decrease the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z]. Finally, subfigure (f) shows the ratio of bare matrix element and renormalization factor в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R, compared with the renormalized matrix element Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z]. ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…Z(z,a)R here is not extracted from a different matrix elements but from в„івҖӢ(z,a)в„ірқ‘§рқ‘ҺM(z,a) itself. This subfigure is a consistency check of our method. In all cases, в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R has little dependence on aрқ‘Һa within error and is consistent with Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z]. Therefore, our self-renormalization method can isolate the residual intrinsic physics from the linear divergence, renormalon uncertainty, log divergence and discretization error with a reasonable precision. Although the ratio в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R has a good behavior for most of the lattice spacings, it may have large errors for small lattice spacings like 0.0318 fm or 0.0574 fm. Therefore we do not recommend using в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R as the renormalized matrix element. Since Exp[gвҖӢ(z)вҲ’m0вҖӢzрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘ҡ0рқ‘§g(z)-m0z] is achieved through our fitting, the large-error data points have little influence on it. We take this as the renormalized matrix element, while using в„івҖӢ(z,a)/ZвҖӢ(z,a)Rв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘Қsubscriptрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘…M(z,a)/Z(z,a)R only to test consistency. We show in Fig. a comparison of renormalized OОіtвҖӢ(z)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘§Ogammat(z) correlation in the pion state between overlap and clover actions. Before we add the systematic error, both results show appreciable difference, which may indicate that two different valence fermions will lead to different results. After we add a small dummy systematic error during the fitting, the correlations in both cases lead to similar results. That means that the systematic error is important, and the residual intrinsic non-perturbative physics is independent of valence quark formulations if it is properly included. Finally, for curiosity, we show in Fig. the renormalized correlations as well as parameters related to the divergence for all cases we studied. As mentioned before, the fitted ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD for overlap quark, overlap pion, clover pion and clover nucleon are similar to each other. But for the clover quark, it is markedly different. We do not yet understand the reason, but it could be due to the combination of chiral symmetry breaking and the linearly-divergent quark mass. We plan to investigate this in the future, but for the time being, at least we understand why we cannot eliminate the linear divergence using RI/MOM for the clover actioncitebib.bib45. Quite surprisingly, the residual intrinsic non-perturbative correlations for overlap quark, pion and nucleon are very similar to each other, which we also do not fully understand.
content_sections.VI Fits with other options and stability: In a phenomenological analysis, the amount of information and the accuracy one can obtain depends, obviously, on the quality of the lattice data. Given the data we have, we would like to push the limit of the analysis by including more physics in the fit until the analysis no longer yields useful information. The first question we would like to address is whether one can get more accurate information about the linear divergence. We have used so far the one-loop perturbative QCD prediction, but treated ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD as a free parameter to partially take into account higher order corrections. However, one might consider directly including at least the second-order corrections. Our strategy here is to fix kрқ‘ҳk to its perturbative value, and to vary ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD and the explicit second order correction characterized by a new parameter О»рқңҶlambda lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=kвҖІвҖӢzaвҖӢОұsвҖӢ(1+О»вҖӢОұs)+gвҖӢ(z)+вҖҰв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘ҳвҖІрқ‘§рқ‘Һsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 1рқңҶsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘”рқ‘§вҖҰdisplaystylelnM(z,a)=frackprimezatildealphas(1+lambdatildealphas)+g(z)+. . . where the terms omitted are the same as those we had before in Eq. . kвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ҳвҖІkprime is related to kрқ‘ҳk by вҲ’2вҖӢПҖ/b02рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ0-2pi/b0 (since вҲ’kвҖІвҖӢ2вҖӢПҖ/b0=ksuperscriptрқ‘ҳвҖІ2рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ0рқ‘ҳ-kprime2pi/b0=k). On the other hand, one might use the О»рқңҶlambda parameter to include some higher order correctionscitebib.bib51, lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=kвҖІвҖӢzaвҖӢОұs1вҲ’О»вҖӢОұs+gвҖӢ(z)+вҖҰв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘ҳвҖІрқ‘§рқ‘Һsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 1рқңҶsubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ рқ‘”рқ‘§вҖҰdisplaystylelnM(z,a)=frackprimezafractildealphas1-lambdatildealphas+g(z)+. . . To be consistent, we now have to use the QCD running coupling constant Оұssubscriptрқӣјрқ‘ tildealphas up to the second order ОІрқӣҪbeta function b1=102вҲ’383вҖӢnfsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ1102383subscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘“b1=102-frac383nf, Оұs=4вҖӢПҖb0вҖӢlnвҒЎ(1a2вҖӢОӣQCD2)вҖӢ(1вҲ’b1b02Г—lnвҒЎ[lnвҒЎ(1a2вҖӢОӣQCD2)]lnвҒЎ(1a2вҖӢОӣQCD2)). subscriptрқӣјрқ‘ 4рқңӢsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ01superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD21subscriptрқ‘Ҹ1superscriptsubscriptрқ‘Ҹ021superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD21superscriptрқ‘Һ2superscriptsubscriptОӣQCD2displaystyletildealphas=frac4pib0ln(frac1a2Lambdarm QCD2)left(1-fracb1b02timesfracln[ln(frac1a2Lambdarm QCD2)]ln(frac1a2Lambdarm QCD2)right). Therefore, again we have two global parameters О»рқңҶlambda and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD in the fit. The fitting process with the above parametrization is similar to what is described in Sec. . The results are shown in Figs. and . As one can see, the range of О»рқңҶlambda and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD are rather large, and strongly correlated. After picking several correlated values of the pair, matching to the perturbative result, the resulting residual matrix elements are shown in the lower panel. One can hardly see any difference between the different choices. Moreover, the results are essentially the same as the fit without the extra О»рқңҶlambda parameter (Fig. ). Therefore, we conclude that with the data at hand, our result is stable with respect to the higher order corrections. Only with more accurate data, one might be able to see the difference of a higher-order fit. Another possibility one can explore is that the higher-order corrections represented by ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD might be different for different ensembles. Therefore, one can discuss different ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD values for MILC and RBC data, lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=kвҖӢzaвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD1,2]+gвҖӢ(z)+f1,2вҖӢ(z)вҖӢaв„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҳрқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCD12рқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘“12рқ‘§рқ‘ҺdisplaystylelnM(z,a)=frackzaln[aLambdarm QCD1,2]+g(z)+f1,2(z)a +3вҖӢCFb0вҖӢlnвҒЎ[lnвҒЎ[1/(aвҖӢОӣQCD1,2)]lnвҒЎ[Ој/ОӣQCD1,2]]+lnвҒЎ[1+dlnвҒЎ(aвҖӢОӣQCD1,2)],3subscriptрқҗ¶рқҗ№subscriptрқ‘Ҹ01рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCD12рқңҮsubscriptОӣQCD121рқ‘‘рқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCD12displaystyle+frac3CFb0ln[fracln[1/(aLambdarm QCD1,2)]ln[mu/Lambdarm QCD1,2]]+ln[1+fracdln(aLambdarm QCD1,2)], where we fix the kрқ‘ҳk parameter and obtain a two parameter fit, ОӣQCD1subscriptОӣQCD1Lambdarm QCD1 and ОӣQCD2subscriptОӣQCD2Lambdarm QCD2. The results of using the above equation is shown in Fig. . As shown, the two parameters are strongly correlated and proportional to each other. The difference of the two is limited to a very small range. Furthermore, with different choices of the correlated pair (ОӣQCD1subscriptОӣQCD1Lambdarm QCD1, ОӣQCD2subscriptОӣQCD2Lambdarm QCD2), the final renormalized results do not change. In Fig. , we show the results of renormalized matrix elements based on different fitting functions. All of these fitting functions lead to the same result. Thus, given the data, it is unnecessary to consider other high-order terms in the fitting function and Eq. is good enough. This also supports the argument made in Sec. that we can use the fitted ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD in leading order to partially represent higher order corrections. Finally, we consider OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) corrections to our fitting formula. These fits were unstable, which indicates that we introduce too many fit parameters for the given data quality. The conclusion is that higher precision data is needed to constrain OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) terms. One can also make fits by including OвҖӢ(a2)рқ‘Ӯsuperscriptрқ‘Һ2O(a2) instead of the linear correction. The physics for doing it is weak in our cases.
content_sections.VII Renormalization of lattice smeared m: In many lattice calculations, the data can be very noisy which calls for more statistics. However, large statistics means more resources which are hard to come by sometimes. Therefore, lattice practitioners have invented phenomenological approaches to quench the short-distance fluctuations: smearing. However, smearing might lead to configurations that are not connected with fundamental theory. On the other hand, smearing in some sense just makes the effective UV cutoff (the inverse of the lattice spacing) smaller, and in the limit aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0ato 0, all smearing becomes local effects which can be taken into account properly through some kind of renormalization. In this section, we check if the procedure we discussed in the previous section still works for smeared matrix elements. To ensure that the HYP smearing data are useful for refined analysis, we can do a simple test on whether the HYP smearing data show the properties of the linear divergence predicted by perturbation theory, as we have done in Sec. . Here we use the following function to fit the bare matrix element в„ів„іM to extract the linear divergence factor, lnвҒЎв„івҖӢ(z,a)=eвҖӢ(z)aвҖӢlnвҒЎ[aвҖӢОӣQCD]+gвҖӢ(z)+f1,2вҖӢ(z)вҖӢa,в„ірқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘’рқ‘§рқ‘Һрқ‘ҺsubscriptОӣQCDрқ‘”рқ‘§subscriptрқ‘“12рқ‘§рқ‘ҺdisplaystylelnM(z,a)=frace(z)aln[aLambdarm QCD]+g(z)+f1,2(z)a, where we allow for a discretization error to get a better fit. We fix ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD at 0.39 GeV in this simple test. Fig. shows the fits for different actions and states which all look reasonable. We have not shown ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 which will be considered in the extraction of the renormalization factor. Fig. shows the extracted eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z) with respect to zрқ‘§z. The linear zрқ‘§z dependence is approximately reproduced but some deviations are seen at large zрқ‘§z. We can perform a linear fitting of eвҖӢ(z)рқ‘’рқ‘§e(z) and compare the fitted slopes. Overall, the quality of the fits is not as good as in the unsmeared cases, although it is still rather impressive. The slope extracted for the linear divergence for the clover quark is totally different from the others, which explains why we cannot use the RI/MOM factor to eliminate it in the clover case for HYP smearing datacitebib.bib45. Although the slopes for the overlap quark and pion, clover pion and nucleon are similar to each other, there are some small discrepancies. Using the RI/MOM factor for these cases may leave small residual linear divergences, and the self-renormalization method provides a better way to renormalize the matrix elements. Next, we extend our self-renormalization method to the HYP smeared data. Our fitting function, Eq. may have no clear physical meanings for the HYP smeared date, but it may be regarded as a phenomenological model. We start with a fit function with two parameters in the linearly divergent part, kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. Fig. shows the ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 map with respect to them. It is clear from the plots that in the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ, we cannot find a solution for kрқ‘ҳk with the one-loop perturbative value 7.4 GeVfm (1.46 if dimensionless, see Eq. ) any more. This is expected because the lattice space is enlarged by a factor of two, and therefore the values of kрқ‘ҳk in the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ are expected to be at most half of this, named 3.7 GeVfm (0.73 if dimensionless). In fact, most probable kрқ‘ҳk is now around 3.0 GeVfm (0.59 if dimensionless) or below. Another way of saying this is HYP smearing smooths out the short-range behaviors such as the linear divergence. The value of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD, on the other hand, has a larger range, possibly corresponding again to a larger effective lattice spacing. So for the HYP smearing cases, we choose a set of (kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD) near the center of the вҖңsmall-ПҮ2superscriptрқң’2chi2 bandвҖқ. We choose k=2.5рқ‘ҳ2.5k=2.5 GeVfm (0.49 if dimensionless) for overlap quark and clover quark, and k=2.6рқ‘ҳ2.6k=2.6 GeVfm (0.51 if dimensionless) for overlap pion, clover pion and clover nucleon. The corresponding ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD shows a larger dispersion, with ОӣQCD=0.43subscriptОӣQCD0.43Lambdarm QCD=0.43 and 0.380.380.38 GeV for overlap quark and pion correlations respectively, and ОӣQCD=0.61subscriptОӣQCD0.61Lambdarm QCD=0.61, 0.370.370.37 and 0.350.350.35 GeV for clover quark, pion and nucleon correlations, respectively. Once the (kрқ‘ҳk, ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD) parameters are chosen, we subtract the linear divergences, and match the result to the perturbative matrix elements to determine possible further subtractions for the non-perturbative mass effect. Fig. shows that the renormalized matrix elements for HYP smearing in blue ticks and lines, in comparison with the cases without HYP smearing. The large difference between smeared and unsmeared matrix elements is seen in the case of clover quark correlation at large zрқ‘§z. Again, we speculate that due to the chiral symmetry breaking effects, the linear divergence has some curious behavior there. However, the difference is smaller in the pion matrix element where the error bars are also bigger. For the overlap case, the correlations for quark and pion show no substantial change due to smearing effects. On the other hand, the matrix element in the nucleon case shows a much smaller error bar in the smeared case, which demonstrates the power of smearing. To conclude, our analysis method to renormalize the linear divergent matrix elements can successfully be used for smeared matrix elements as well. Smearing does not seem to change the behavior of renormalization qualitatively, but only quantitatively. The intrinsic physics does not seem to change under (moderate) smearing, consistent with the expectation that smearing is an alternative method to reduce the statistical noise in lattice calculations.
content_sections.VIII Linear Divergence of Correlations i: In this section, we analyze the linear divergence of the Wilson line in the matrix elements taken in the QCD vacuum. We try to test if the divergence is universal and if the divergent mass can be extracted successfully from these matrix elements. We consider three new types of matrix elements: 1) large-size Wilson loop that has been used to extract heavy-quark potential, 2) the vacuum matrix element of the quasi-PDF operator, 3) the vacuum matrix element of Wilson link operator in a fixed gauge. The data are for MILC ensembles and, when applicable, with the clover action. According to the last section, smearing does not change the form of renormalization, and therefore, we consider here the higher-precision data with one step smearing. Since we just want to test the linear divergence, we can use the simple function Eq. in our fitting.

To extract the linear divergence from lattice data, a Wilson loop is a good choice because it is easy to calculate, gauge invariant, and has high precision. It has been extensively studied in the literaturecitebib.bib43, bib.bib27, bib.bib59, bib.bib38, bib.bib28, particularly in relation to the heavy quark potential. In our analysis, we will not consider the heavy quark potential interpretation but simply consider a Wilson loop as a matrix element and fit its 1/a1рқ‘Һ1/a divergence by looking at the logarithm of the matrix element, as shown in Fig. . The lattice spacing dependence has been fitted well with our formula with a choice of ОӣQCD=0.39subscriptОӣQCD0.39Lambdarm QCD=0.39 GeV, which is a value favored by our fitting in the previous section. After isolating the linear divergent coefficient, we plot it as a function of r+tрқ‘ҹрқ‘Ўr+t, for which half of the slope gives us the divergent coefficient. Our value is about 2.5 GeVfm (0.49 if dimensionless). This value is very similar to the value we found in the previous section (Fig. ), demonstrating that the linear divergence can be very well extracted from the Wilson loop.

Here we consider the vacuum matrix elements of a quasi-PDF operator which were suggested as choices for the renormalization factor in Ref. citebib.bib44. One can consider the vacuum expectation value (VEV) of OОіtвҖӢ(t)subscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘ЎOgammat(t). As a gauge invariant choice proposed in Ref. citebib.bib44. вҹЁOО“вҖӢ(t)вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘Ў OGamma(t) can be obtained through the following stochastic estimation: вҹЁOО“вҖӢ(t)вҹ©=1Ls3вҖӢвҹЁвҲ‘xвҶ’TrвҖӢ[UвҖӢ(xвҶ’,0;xвҶ’,t)вҖӢО“вҖӢSwвҖӢ(xвҶ’,t)]вҹ©delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptрқ‘ӮО“рқ‘Ў1superscriptsubscriptрқҗҝрқ‘ 3delimited-вҹЁвҹ©subscriptвҶ’рқ‘ҘTrdelimited-рқ‘ҲвҶ’рқ‘Ҙ0вҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎО“subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ӨвҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ўdisplaystyle OGamma(t)=frac1Ls3sumvecxtextrmTr[U(vecx,0;vecx,t)Gamma Sw(vecx,t)] where the second term on the right hand side of the second line vanishes as it is not gauge invariant, and SwвҖӢ(xвҶ’,t)=вҲ‘yвҶ’SвҖӢ(xвҶ’,t;yвҶ’,0)subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ӨвҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎsubscriptвҶ’рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҶвҶ’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ЎвҶ’рқ‘Ұ0Sw(vecx,t)=sumvecyS(vecx,t;vecy,0) is a wall source quark propagator without gauge fixing. Such a proposal can only be applied for the non-vanishing cases with О“=ОіtО“subscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎGamma=gammat or в„җв„җcal I. We will apply it to OОіtsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎOgammat. However, it is simple to see that the OОіtsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎOgammat matrix element in the vacuum vanishes at small tрқ‘Ўt using operator product expansion. Therefore the matrix element is susceptible to large O(aрқ‘Һa) correction. Only at very large tрқ‘Ўt, one might see the correct slope. Results of a test of the linear divergence for the vacuum matrix element of the PDF operator is shown in Fig. . The slope at large tрқ‘Ўt appears to be consistent with that from the Wilson loop case but only within uninterestingly large errors.

For the gauge-dependent matrix elements, we consider the Wilson line in Landau gauge as the simplest choice. The result of our test of the linear divergence is shown in Fig. . At small-zрқ‘§z, where perturbation theory works well, the slope roughly agrees with the prediction from perturbation theory. For large-zрқ‘§z, the matrix element does not have a transfer-matrix interpretation in this gauge. Finally, Fig. is a comparison of the linearly divergent term for Wilson Loop, VEV and Wilson link. At small zрқ‘§z, the linear divergence terms of Wilson Loop and Wilson link are consistent while at large zрқ‘§z they disagree strongly. The linear divergence term of VEV is different from the others because the large discretization errors make the extraction of the linear divergence very imprecise.
content_sections.IX Summary: The proper renormalization of quasi-LF correlations calculated on the lattice has been a main challenge for the applications of LaMET to studying parton physics. Such correlations contain linear divergences associated with Wilson lines which have to be eliminated with high precision to extract the desired physics information. In this work, we propose a self-renormalization method to eliminate both the linear and the logarithmic divergence, as well as the discretization error, from a quasi-LF matrix element which can be matched to a continuum scheme at short distance. As a paradigmatic example, we show that our method works well for OОіtsubscriptрқ‘Ӯsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎOgammat matrix elements in the pion, nucleon, and off-shell quark state. Our analysis shows that the renormalization factors are universal in the hadron state considered. Moreover, the renormalized correlations in the pion state for clover and overlap fermions are similar to each other. Besides, We find a large non-perturbative effect in the popular RI/MOM and ratio renormalization scheme used previously, which has to and can be avoided in the hybrid renormalization scheme proposed recently. It is certainly interesting to see that our method works also for HYP smeared matrix elements. This paves the way to do a phenomenological renormalization for smeared high-precision lattice data. For the convenience of the reader, we collect the parameters related to renormalization for various matrix elements discussed in the previous sections, see Tables and. For HYP smearing cases, there is much freedom for the choice of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD. We choose ОӣQCD=0.39subscriptОӣQCD0.39Lambdarm QCD=0.39 GeV in Table. But we also find that ОӣQCD=0.1subscriptОӣQCD0.1Lambdarm QCD=0.1 GeV gives us smaller fitted discretization errors. For comparison purposes, we also show results based on 2+1 flavor clover quark and Luescher-Weisz (equivalent to Symanzik) gauge ensembles from CLS collaborationcitebib.bib71. We choose valence fermion action to be clover action, the same as sea fermion action. The renormalization parameters for CLS ensembles are shown in Table. The parameters kрқ‘ҳk and ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCD for pion matrix elements for CLS ensembles are similar to those for MILC and RBC ensembles, suggesting consistency between results using mixed actions or not. We would like to stress that the application of our method is not limited to the matrix elements discussed in this paper. Instead, it can be applied to any matrix element of an operator in the form of Eq. . It can also be generalized, in principle, to any matrix element with a Wilson line in LaMET applicationscitebib.bib8. Of course, in these cases one also needs to consider the physical interpretations of the matrix elements and the details related to lattice calculations carefully.
