sorted_papers.title:
Renormalization of asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line operators in
  lattice and continuum perturbation theory
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this work, we study the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear
operators containing an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line at the one-loop
level in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory. These operators enter
the first-principle calculation of transverse momentum-dependent parton
distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD using the formulation of Large
Momentum Effective Theory. We provide appropriate RI$'$-type conditions that
address the power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing among
staple operators of different Dirac structures, using a number of different
possible projectors. A variant of RI$'$, including calculations of rectangular
Wilson loops, which cancel the pinch-pole singularities of the staple operators
at infinite length and reduce residual power divergences, is also employed. We
calculate at one-loop order the conversion matrix, which relates the
quasi-TMDPDFs in the RI$'$-type schemes to the reference scheme $\overline{\rm
MS}$ for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and of the
dimensions of the staple.

sections.2: Cohen, В andВ XiangdongВ Ji,В вҖңFlavor Structure of the Nucleon Sea from Lattice QCD,вҖқВ Phys
sections.3: Anderle etВ al.,В вҖңElectron-ion collider in China,вҖқВ Front
sections.4: Accardi etВ al.,В вҖңElectron Ion Collider: The Next QCD Frontier: Understanding the glue that binds us all,вҖқВ Eur
sections.10: Brandt, FilippoВ Neri, В andВ Masa-akiВ Sato,В вҖңRenormalization of Loop Functions for All Loops,вҖқВ Phys

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "Asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line operators exhibit linear divergences in both lattice and continuum perturbation theory, which must be properly renormalized for accurate first-principles calculations of transverse momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDPDFs) in lattice QCD.",
    "The renormalization of these operators requires handling both power-law and logarithmic divergences, as well as mixing among different Dirac structures, necessitating the use of RI'-type renormalization conditions with multiple projectors.",
    "A variant of the RI' scheme incorporating rectangular Wilson loops is introduced to cancel pinch-pole singularities that arise at infinite staple length and to reduce residual power divergences, improving the renormalization procedure.",
    "The conversion matrix between the quasi-TMDPDFs in the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is computed at one-loop order, enabling a consistent matching between lattice and continuum frameworks.",
    "The renormalization procedure is valid for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and the dimensions of the staple, ensuring flexibility in practical lattice calculations."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The conversion matrix between the RI'-type schemes and the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme is computed at one-loop order, with explicit dependence on the renormalization momentum scale $\\mu$ and the staple dimensions $L_1$, $L_2$.",
    "The renormalization factor for the staple operator includes a logarithmic divergence proportional to $\\ln(\\mu L_1)$ and $\\ln(\\mu L_2)$, where $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the lengths of the staple legs.",
    "The inclusion of rectangular Wilson loops in the RI' variant leads to cancellation of pinch-pole singularities in the limit $L_1, L_2 \\to \\infty$, with residual power divergences reduced to $\\mathcal{O}(1/\\mu)$.",
    "The one-loop renormalization constants for the staple operators are derived using a set of projectors $\\Gamma_i$ corresponding to different Dirac structures, with mixing captured by a matrix $Z_{ij}$.",
    "The final renormalized quasi-TMDPDF is related to the $\overline{\rm MS}$-renormalized TMDPDF via $\\mathcal{O}(\\alpha_s)$ corrections, with the conversion matrix $C_{\\text{RI}' \\to \\overline{\\rm MS}}$ explicitly computed as a function of $\\mu$, $L_1$, and $L_2$."
  ]
}

authors: Gregoris Spanoudes, Martha Constantinou, Haralambos Panagopoulos

content_sections.I Introduction: One of the directions of research in lattice QCD, which has shown rapid progress in the last decade, is the first-principle study of a family of nonperturbative distribution functions (DFs) that describe the internal structure of hadrons: parton distribution functions (PDFs), generalized parton distribution functions (GPDs) and transverse-momentum dependent parton distributions functions (TMDPDFs). All three types of DFs are crucial for a comprehensive understanding of the three-dimensional hadron picture. Calculating DFs from first principles has long been a challenge in Hadron Physics due to their nonperturbative and light-cone nature. The latter does not allow for a direct nonperturbative computation of DFs on Euclidean lattice. In the last decade, a groundbreaking approach by X. Jicitebib.bib1 has overcome this issue. The approach connects Euclidean equal-time correlation functions (referred to as quasi-DFs), which are accessible by lattice simulations, to the physical light-cone DFs using the framework of Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET)citebib.bib2. This breakthrough has paved the way for extracting, for the first time, DFs from lattice simulations. Several groups have successfully employed this approach in the calculation of various quark and gluon DFs on the lattice: quark PDFs and GPDs in Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, quark TMDPDFs and soft-function in Refs. citebib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, gluon PDFs in Refs. citebib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78 and hadronic light-cone distribution amplitudes (DA) in Refs. citebib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib7, bib.bib81, bib.bib82. An overview of recent progress in the research of DFs on the lattice can be found in Refs. citebib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87. The goal of these studies is to complement the planned experimental programs for investigating the 3D tomography of the nucleon in major experimental facilities, such as the electron ion colliders of USAcitebib.bib88, bib.bib89 and Chinacitebib.bib90. Theoretical studies can significantly complement the experimental investigations, especially when there are limitations either in the experimental programs or in the phenomenological models used for the analysis of the experimental data. The computation of DFs using JiвҖҷs approach (also called the вҖңquasi-PDFsвҖқ approach) involves a three-step process. Firstly, nonperturbative calculations are performed to determine hadron matrix elements of gauge-invariant quark or gluon nonlocal operators. These operators contain path-ordered Wilson lines with specific shapes, such as straight lines for PDFs and staple-shaped lines for TMDPDFs. Secondly, the nonlocal operators are renormalized to establish a connection with physically measurable quantities. This task is challenging compared to the case of local operators, as explained below. Lastly, the renormalized lattice DFs are perturbatively matched to the corresponding physical light-cone DFs using LaMET. One-loop matching formulae have been extracted in the literature for several quasi DFscitebib.bib91, bib.bib92; in some cases a two-loop formula is also availablecitebib.bib93, bib.bib94. In this study, we focus on the implementation of the second step in the quasi-PDFs approach regarding the renormalization of nonlocal operators using one-loop perturbation theory in both continuum and lattice regularizations. While a nonperturbative calculation of the renormalization functions in numerical simulations of lattice QCD is desirable, perturbation theory can give important feedback for the development of an appropriate nonperturbative renormalization prescription, which addresses all kinds of divergences, as well as possible mixing with operators of equal or lower dimension allowed by global symmetries. Most importantly, perturbation theory gives us the matching functions (at a given order) between nonperturbative renormalization schemes and continuum perturbative schemes вҖ“ primarily MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS вҖ“ employed in phenomenology. Studies of nonlocal operators with Wilson lines in continuum perturbation theory go back decades, including seminal workcitebib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib97, bib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101, bib.bib102, bib.bib103, bib.bib104 for the renormalization of open and closed (loops) Wilson lines, with and without singular points (cusps, self-intersections), and having quark or gluon fields at the endpoints. Lattice studies of nonlocal operators have emerged only in the last decade after the development of the quasi-PDFs approach. The first perturbative lattice calculation of Wilson-line operators was made by our group in Ref. citebib.bib105, to one loop for massless quarks, using the Wilson/clover fermion action and a variety of Symanzik-improved gluon actions. A straight Wilson line with quark fields at the endpoints was employed in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-PDFs. This study showed that the lattice formulation introduces several new complications, such as mixing among operators of equal dimension and different twists, and power-law divergences (even in the absence of mixing with lower-dimensional operators, in contrast to the case of local operators). A number of extensions of this study have been followed by our group regarding the presence of finite quark masscitebib.bib106 and the calculation of one-loop artifacts to all orders in the lattice spacing aрқ‘Һacitebib.bib107. In a different extension of our study, we have considered nonlocal operators with a symmetric staple-shaped Wilson linecitebib.bib108 in order to investigate the renormalization of quark quasi-TMDPDFs. In our current work, we extend further the latter calculation by employing the more general case of an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line, where all three segments of the staple can have different lengths. Studies by other groups along these lines have appeared in Refs. citebib.bib109, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112. Staple-shaped nonlocal operators have a wide range of applicability. They enter the analysis of semi-inclusive deep inelastic scattering (SIDIS) processes, as well as the Drell-Yan (DY) processes, in a kinematic region where the photon virtuality is large and the measured transverse momentum of the produced hadron is of the order of ОӣQCDsubscriptОӣQCDLambdarm QCDcitebib.bib113. In these analyses, the segments of the staple that are parallel to the direction in which the hadron is boosted have an infinite length. Thus, while our study focuses on finite lengths of the staple segments, an extrapolation to infinite limit must be taken in the renormalized matrix elements of these operators. The presence or not of an asymmetry in the shape of the staple operators affects their renormalization. Besides the presence of an additional scale in the renormalization conditions, the mixing pattern is different between symmetric and asymmetric staple operators (see Sec. ). However, this difference is not visible in one-loop lattice perturbation theory, as concluded by the present calculation. As stated before, there are a number of challenges to address in order to renormalize the nonlocal Wilson-line operators of arbitrary shape: 1. Power divergences arise for cutoff regularized theories, such as lattice QCDcitebib.bib98. The divergences depend on the total length (LрқҗҝL) of the Wilson line and can be absorbed in an exponential factor of the form eвҲ’ОҙвҖӢmвҖӢLsuperscriptрқ‘’рқӣҝрқ‘ҡрқҗҝe-delta mL, where ОҙвҖӢmрқӣҝрқ‘ҡdelta m is a dimensionful regularization-dependent quantity whose magnitude diverges linearly with the regulator. 2. Logarithmic divergences arise not only from contact terms but also from the singular points of the Wilson line. In the case of a straight line, singular points are just the endpoints. For operators of different shapes, there can also be cusp divergencescitebib.bib100, which depend on the angle and number of cusps present. In the case of the staple line, there are two cusps of angle ПҖ/2рқңӢ2pi/2. These divergences can be addressed by using typical renormalization schemes, such as RI or ratio schemes. 3. Finite operator mixing arises between Wilson-line operators рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma with different (products of) Dirac matrices О“О“Gamma depending on the regularization. In the case of straight-line operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib105 shows mixing in pairs between nonlocal fermion bilinears of the form (рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma, рқ’ӘО“,ОіОҪ1/2subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ12OGamma,gammanu1/2), where ОҪ1subscriptрқңҲ1hatnu1 is the direction of the straight line, for chirality-breaking actions. Symmetry arguments (reflections, charge conjugation)citebib.bib114 confirm that the one-loop mixing pattern is also valid nonperturbatively. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, the one-loop computationcitebib.bib108 shows mixing in pairs with a different pattern: (рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma, рқ’Ә[О“,ОіОҪ2]/2subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ22O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2), where ОҪ2subscriptрқңҲ2hatnu2 is the direction of the sides of the staple line. However, symmetry argumentscitebib.bib68 show a wider mixing pattern: quadruplets of operators emerge in the asymmetric case and triplets in the symmetric case when nonchiral fermions are employed. 4. In the case of (symmetric and asymmetric) staple operators, a pinch-pole singularitycitebib.bib57 arises when one takes the infinite limit of lateral sizes of the staple (this limit is included in the definition of TMDPDFs). This singularity вҖ“ a linear divergence вҖ“ comes from the gluon exchange between the two parallel (вҖңlongitudinalвҖқ) segments of the staple. 5. The renormalization functions are (in general) complex in nonminimal subtraction schemes1The presence of an imaginary part in the renormalization functions depends on the exact definition of the renormalization scheme. For example, one can define purely real renormalization conditions by using only the real part of вҖңprojectedвҖқ GreenвҖҷs functions; in this way, both the real and the imaginary part of the renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions will be rendered finite. . Nonperturbative renormalization prescriptionscitebib.bib115, bib.bib8, bib.bib116, bib.bib58, bib.bib65, bib.bib68 have already been employed in lattice simulations for both straight-line and staple operators using RI-type schemes. There are also studies of alternative prescriptions, such as the ratio (or short-distance ratio) schemecitebib.bib66, Wilson-line-mass-subtraction schemecitebib.bib117, bib.bib109, RI-xMOM scheme (using the auxiliary-field approachcitebib.bib103, bib.bib109, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, bib.bib120) and a hybrid renormalization schemecitebib.bib121. While a standard RI-type scheme (extended in the presence of mixing) can treat the various types of divergences and mixing of the nonlocal operators at one loop, recent nonperturbative examinationscitebib.bib122, bib.bib66 at different lattice spacings have provided compelling evidence indicating the existence of residual linear divergences in both straight-line and staple operators. In this regard, an alternative version of RI, as suggested in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib65, which includes the computation of vacuum expectation value for rectangular Wilson loops, is also employed in our current work. This prescription (described in Sec. ) is expected to suppress the residual power divergences from the GreenвҖҷs functions of the staple operators (see Sec. ). The paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we provide the setup of our calculation, including the definition of the operators under study and the renormalization conditions that we employ throughout, corresponding to four different variants of the RI scheme. Sections and present our main results in dimensional and lattice regularization, respectively. This includes both the renormalization functions and conversion matrices between the RI-type and MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS schemes. In Sec. , we summarize and give some future plans. We have also included three appendices. In Appendix, we provide a list of formulae for the calculation, in dimensional regularization, of one-loop Feynman integrals appropriate to nonlocal operators. Appendix contains the definitions of Feynman-parameter integrals appearing in our results. In Appendix, we collect one-loop results for the renormalization of the quark field in both dimensional and lattice regularizations.

For example, one can match our convention to Ref. citebib.bib68 through yвҶ’lвҶ’рқ‘Ұрқ‘ҷyrightarrow l, zвҶ’bвҶ’рқ‘§рқ‘Ҹzrightarrow b, and (yвҲ’yвҖІ)вҶ’вҲ’zвҶ’рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқ‘§(y-yprime)rightarrow-z. . The asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson-line fermion bilinear operators are defined in Euclidean space as follows: рқ’ӘО“вҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)вүЎПҲВҜвҖӢ(x)вҖӢО“вҖӢрқ’ІвҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)вҖӢПҲвҖӢ(x+zвҖӢОҪ1+(yвҲ’yвҖІ)вҖӢОҪ2),subscriptрқ’ӘО“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІВҜрқң“рқ‘ҘО“рқ’Ірқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқң“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2OGamma(x,z,y,yprime)equivbarpsi(x) Gamma W(x,z,y,yprime) psi(x+zhatnu1+(y-yprime)hatnu2), (II.1.1) where рқ’ІвҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)рқ’Ірқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІW(x,z,y,yprime) denotes the staple-shaped Wilson line as given schematically in Fig. and defined as: рқ’ІвҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)рқ’Ірқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІdisplaystyleW(x,z,y,yprime) вүЎdisplaystyleequiv рқ’°вҖӢ(x,yвҖӢОҪ2)вҖӢрқ’°вҖӢ(x+yвҖӢОҪ2,zвҖӢОҪ1)вҖӢрқ’°вҖ вҖӢ(x+zвҖӢОҪ1+(yвҲ’yвҖІ)вҖӢОҪ2,yвҖІвҖӢОҪ2),рқ’°рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰsubscriptрқңҲ2рқ’°рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҰsubscriptрқңҲ2рқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1superscriptрқ’°вҖ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2superscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2displaystyleU(x,y,hatnu2) U(x+y,hatnu2,z,hatnu1) Udagger(x+z,hatnu1+(y-yprime),hatnu2,yprime,hatnu2), (II.1.2) рқ’°вҖӢ(r,в„“вҖӢОј)рқ’°рқ‘ҹв„“рқңҮdisplaystyleU(r,ell,hatmu) вүЎdisplaystyleequiv рқ’«вҖӢexpвҒЎ[iвҖӢgвҖӢвҲ«0в„“рқ‘‘в„“ВҜвҖӢрқ’ңОјвҖӢ(r+в„“ВҜвҖӢОј)]. рқ’«рқ‘–рқ‘”superscriptsubscript0в„“differential-dВҜв„“subscriptрқ’ңрқңҮрқ‘ҹВҜв„“рқңҮdisplaystylePexpbigg[igint0elldbarell Amu(r+barell,hatmu)bigg]. (II.1.3) рқ’°вҖӢ(r,в„“вҖӢОј)рқ’°рқ‘ҹв„“рқңҮU(r,ell,hatmu) denotes the straight-line path-ordered (рқ’«рқ’«P) exponential (Wilson line), expressed in terms of the gluon field рқ’ңОјsubscriptрқ’ңрқңҮAmu, which connects the points rрқ‘ҹr and r+в„“вҖӢОјрқ‘ҹв„“рқңҮr+ellhatmu. A lattice discretization of рқ’°вҖӢ(r,в„“вҖӢОј)рқ’°рқ‘ҹв„“рқңҮU(r,ell hatmu) in terms of gluon links UОјвҖӢ(x)subscriptрқ‘ҲрқңҮрқ‘ҘUmu(x) which connect points xрқ‘Ҙx and x+aвҖӢОјрқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҺрқңҮx+ahatmu is given below: рқ’°вҖӢ(r,в„“вҖӢОј)=вҲҸв„“ВҜ=0в„“вҲ“1UВұОјвҖӢ(r+в„“ВҜвҖӢaвҖӢОј),рқ’°рқ‘ҹв„“рқңҮsuperscriptsubscriptproductВҜв„“0minus-or-plusв„“1subscriptрқ‘Ҳplus-or-minusрқңҮрқ‘ҹВҜв„“рқ‘ҺрқңҮU(r,ell hatmu)=prodbarell=0ellmp 1Upmmu(r+barellahatmu), (II.1.4) where UвҲ’ОјвҖӢ(x)вүЎUОјвҖ вҖӢ(xвҲ’aвҖӢОј)subscriptрқ‘ҲрқңҮрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptрқ‘ҲрқңҮвҖ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘ҺрқңҮU-mu(x)equiv Umudagger(x-ahatmu) and upper (lower) signs correspond to в„“>0в„“0ell>0 (в„“<0в„“0ell<0). Other discretizations involve smeared gluon links, e. g. , stout, HYP, Wilson flow. We plan to investigate the impact of stout smearing at the one-loop level in future work. There is a total of 16 different operators which can be extracted from Eq. depending on the choice of the Dirac matrix О“О“Gamma inserted between the fermion and antifermion fields: О“=рқҹҷО“рқҹҷGamma=openone (scalar Sрқ‘ҶS), Оі5вүЎОі1вҖӢОі2вҖӢОі3вҖӢОі4subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫ1subscriptрқӣҫ2subscriptрқӣҫ3subscriptрқӣҫ4gamma5equivgamma1gamma2gamma3gamma4 (pseudoscalar Pрқ‘ғP), ОіОјsubscriptрқӣҫрқңҮgammamu (vector VОјsubscriptрқ‘үрқңҮVmu), Оі5вҖӢОіОјsubscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫрқңҮgamma5gammamu (axial vector AОјsubscriptрқҗҙрқңҮAmu), ПғОјвҖӢОҪвүЎ[ОіОј,ОіОҪ]/2subscriptрқңҺрқңҮрқңҲsubscriptрқӣҫрқңҮsubscriptрқӣҫрқңҲ2sigmamunuequiv[gammamu,gammanu]/2 (tensor TОјвҖӢОҪsubscriptрқ‘ҮрқңҮрқңҲTmunu). Of particular interest is the study of vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators, which correspond to the unpolarized, helicity, and transversity types of TMDPDFs, respectively. Since we are working in one-loop perturbation theory with massless fermions, the flavor content of the fermion fields ПҲрқң“psi and ПҲВҜВҜрқң“barpsi is irrelevant for our calculations up to this order. Thus, our one-loop results are valid for both flavor singlet and nonsinglet operators, given that there is no additional mixing with gluon nonlocal operatorscitebib.bib119, bib.bib70.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.2 Symmetry prope: We examine below the properties of staple operators under symmetry transformations. Similar investigations can be found incitebib.bib65, bib.bib68. Operators with the same behavior under all symmetries can mix among themselves. In this way, one can identify the mixing pattern of the staple operators based on nonperturbative arguments. Since the staple operators depend on two special directions (ОҪ1subscriptрқңҲ1hatnu1 and ОҪ2subscriptрқңҲ2hatnu2) in which the staple is defined, we consider appropriate versions of the symmetry transformations of the QCD action (in Euclidean space) with respect to the special directions. 1. Translational symmetry: The operators are covariant under translations in Euclidean space. Similarly to the case of local operators рқ’ӘвҖӢ(x)рқ’Әрқ‘Ҙcal O(x) which cannot mix with translated versions of themselves (рқ’ӘвҖӢ(y)рқ’Әрқ‘Ұcal O(y)), mixing among nonlocal operators involving different paths and shapes for the Wilson line joining the fermion-antifermion pair cannot occurcitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. 2. Two-dimensional (2D) rotational (or square, on the lattice) symmetry: The operators are covariant under rotations over the two-dimensional (2D) plane transverse to the plane in which the staple is defined. The 16 staple operators are classified into two representations of the 2D rotational group: scalar: S,P,VОҪ1,VОҪ2,AОҪ1,AОҪ2,TОҪ1вҖӢОҪ2,TОҪ3вҖӢОҪ4рқ‘Ҷрқ‘ғsubscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ4S,P,Vnu1,Vnu2,Anu1,Anu2,Tnu1nu2,Tnu3nu4, vector: (VОҪ3,VОҪ4),(AОҪ3,AОҪ4),(TОҪ3вҖӢОҪ1,TОҪ4вҖӢОҪ1),(TОҪ3вҖӢОҪ2,TОҪ4вҖӢОҪ2)subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқ‘ҮsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҲ2(Vnu3,Vnu4),(Anu3,Anu4),(Tnu3nu1,Tnu4nu1),(Tnu3nu2,Tnu4nu2), where (ОҪ1,ОҪ2,ОҪ3,ОҪ4)subscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ4(nu1,nu2,nu3,nu4) correspond to different orthogonal directions in the 4D Euclidean space. The first representation is one-dimensional while the second is two-dimensional reducible (e. g. , (VОҪ3,VОҪ4)subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ4(Vnu3,Vnu4) splits into two different one-dimensional representations: VОҪ3+iвҖӢVОҪ4subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ3рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ4Vnu3+iVnu4 and VОҪ3вҲ’iвҖӢVОҪ4subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ3рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ4Vnu3-iVnu4). Operator mixing can only occur among operators that support the same irreducible representation. The fact that an operator such as VОҪ3+iвҖӢVОҪ4subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ3рқ‘–subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ4Vnu3+iVnu4 can mix with AОҪ3+iвҖӢAОҪ4subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ3рқ‘–subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ4Anu3+iAnu4 but not with AОҪ3вҲ’iвҖӢAОҪ4subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ3рқ‘–subscriptрқҗҙsubscriptрқңҲ4Anu3-iAnu4 implies certain relations among the corresponding renormalization and mixing coefficients. Thus, in contrast to the local operators, which are covariant under 4D rotations, the residual rotational symmetry cannot completely prevent mixing between scalar, pseudoscalar, vector, axial-vector, and tensor operators. Also, operators which support the same representation of the 4D rotational group but different representations of the 2D rotational group, e. g. VОҪ1subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ1Vnu1 (or VОҪ2subscriptрқ‘үsubscriptрқңҲ2Vnu2) and VПҒвү (ОҪ1,ОҪ2)subscriptрқ‘үрқңҢsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқңҲ2Vrhoneq(nu1,nu2), will not share the same renormalization factor (in contrast to the case of local operators); at least, the 2D rotational symmetry prevents the mixing among these operators. 3. We conclude that when the fermion action breaks chiral symmetry (e. g. , Wilson/clover fermions, twisted-mass fermions), mixing among the asymmetric staple operators (рқ’ӘО“,рқ’ӘО“вҖӢОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2,рқ’ӘО“вҖӢОіОҪ1,рқ’ӘО“вҖӢОіОҪ2)subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2,OGammagammanu1,OGammagammanu2) is allowed. When chiral fermions (e. g. , massless overlap/domain-wall/continuum fermions) are employed, the mixing pattern is minimized to (рқ’ӘО“,рқ’ӘО“вҖӢОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2)subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2(OGamma,OGammagammanu1gammanu2). In the specific case of symmetric staple operators (yвҖІ=ysuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқ‘Ұyprime=y), the basis of independent operators is reduced (рқ’Әi=0subscriptрқ’Әрқ‘–0widetildeOi=0, for i>4рқ‘–4i>4) and the mixing pattern takes the form: (рқ’ӘО“,рқ’Ә[О“,ОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2]/2,рқ’Ә[О“,ОіОҪ1]/2,рқ’Ә[О“,ОіОҪ2]/2)subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ22subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ12subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu1]/2,O[Gamma,gammanu2]/2) for nonchiral fermions and (рқ’ӘО“,рқ’Ә[О“,ОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2]/2)subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ22(OGamma,O[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]/2) for chiral fermions. Depending on the Dirac matrix О“О“Gamma, one or three out of the three commutators [О“,ОіОҪ1]О“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1[Gamma,gammanu1], [О“,ОіОҪ2]О“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2[Gamma,gammanu2], [О“,ОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2]О“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2] appearing in the mixing pattern of the symmmetric staple operators with nonchiral fermions will be zero, thus leading to a mixing triplet or singlet (multiplicatively renormalizable operator), respectively. Similarly, when [О“,ОіОҪ1вҖӢОіОҪ2]вү 0О“subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ20[Gamma,gammanu1gammanu2]neq 0, the mixing pattern of the symmetric staple operators with chiral fermions gives a mixing pair; otherwise it leads to a multiplicatively renormalizable operator. In contrast to the symmetric case, symmetry arguments alone are not sufficient to establish multiplicative renormalization for any asymmetric operator. However, all-order perturbative studies in the continuum show that a multiplicative renormalization can address all the divergences of the nonlocal Wilson-line operatorscitebib.bib98, bib.bib100, bib.bib103. Thus, the mixing which is allowed by symmetries is absent in minimal subtraction schemes. However, it can occur as finite mixing in nonminimal schemes. On the lattice, the mixing is present when employing MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS or any nonperturbative intermediate scheme; while MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS is a minimal continuum scheme, on the lattice, additional finite regularization-dependent terms contribute to the renormalization functions in order to be able to match the continuum MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions. In our work, we focus on the wider mixing pattern arising in the case of asymmetric staple operators when a chirality-breaking fermion action is employed. Our goal is to construct a common prescription for renormalizing the staple operators in any regularization (regularization independent). To this end, we consider the following quadruplets of the sixteen independent bilinear operators рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma (with О“вҲҲSiО“subscriptрқ‘Ҷрқ‘–Gammain Si)3There is freedom in choosing the signs in front of each operator, leading to different conventions. : S1subscriptрқ‘Ҷ1displaystyle S1 вүЎdisplaystyleequiv рқҹҷ,ПғОҪрқҹҷвҖӢОҪрқҹҡ,ОіОҪрқҹҷ,ОіОҪрқҹҡ,displaystyle openone , sigmanu1nu2, gammanu1 , gammanu2 , (II.2.33) S2subscriptрқ‘Ҷ2displaystyle S2 вүЎdisplaystyleequiv Оі5,ПғОҪ4вҖӢОҪ3,Оі5вҖӢОіОҪ1,Оі5вҖӢОіОҪ2,subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқңҺsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2displaystyle gamma5 , sigmanu4nu3, gamma5gammanu1, gamma5gammanu2 , (II.2.34) S3subscriptрқ‘Ҷ3displaystyle S3 вүЎdisplaystyleequiv ОіОҪ3,Оі5вҖӢОіОҪ4,ПғОҪ3вҖӢОҪ1,ПғОҪ3вҖӢОҪ2,subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҺsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқңҺsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҲ2displaystyle gammanu3, gamma5gammanu4, sigmanu3nu1, sigmanu3nu2 , (II.2.35) S4subscriptрқ‘Ҷ4displaystyle S4 вүЎdisplaystyleequiv ОіОҪ4,Оі5вҖӢОіОҪ3,ПғОҪ4вҖӢОҪ1,ПғОҪ4вҖӢОҪ2. subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқӣҫ5subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ3subscriptрқңҺsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқңҺsubscriptрқңҲ4subscriptрқңҲ2displaystyle gammanu4, gamma5gammanu3, sigmanu4nu1, sigmanu4nu2 . (II.2.36) In the present study, we do not consider possible mixing (on the lattice) with higher dimensional operators multiplied by the appropriate power of the lattice spacing. In the case of local operators, such mixing is present only for finite values of the lattice spacing, and it vanishes when taking the continuum limit. However, in the case of nonlocal operators, where power divergences aвҲ’n,nвҲҲв„Ө+superscriptрқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘ӣsuperscriptв„Өa-n, ninZ+ are present, рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a)рқ’Әрқ‘ҺO(a) effects in the bare GreenвҖҷs functions can contribute to the renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions at two loops4рқ’ӘвҖӢ(aвҖӢg2)рқ’Әрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”2O(a g2) terms from the bare GreenвҖҷs function multiplied by рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1/aвҖӢg2)рқ’Ә1рқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘”2O(1/a g2) terms from the renormalization function lead to рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a0вҖӢg4)рқ’Әsuperscriptрқ‘Һ0superscriptрқ‘”4O(a0 g4) contributions to the renormalized GreenвҖҷs function. . Alternatively, one can suppress these unwanted effects in two ways: by removing power divergences from the GreenвҖҷs function through an appropriate ratio with another GreenвҖҷs function which has the same power divergences, e. g. , a closed Wilson loop, by subtracting artifacts from the bare GreenвҖҷs functions calculated in lattice perturbation theory. Our group has successfully applied this method to the renormalization of local quark bilinear operatorscitebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126, and more recently to the renormalization of nonlocal straight Wilson-line operators for quasi-PDFscitebib.bib107. We plan to study one-loop discretization effects for the staple operators to all orders in the lattice spacing in future work.
content_sections.II Calculation setup II.3 GreenвҖҷs func: As is standard practice, one-particle-irreducible (1-PI) two-point amputated GreenвҖҷs functions of the operators under study with external elementary fields, e. g. , fermion fields, can be used for the extraction of renormalization functions 5The removal of the standard momentum-space delta functions which appear in GreenвҖҷs functions such as Eq. and Eq. is understood. : ОӣО“вҖӢ(q,z,y,yвҖІ)=вҲ‘xвҹЁПҲвҖӢ(q)|рқ’ӘО“вҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)|ПҲВҜвҖӢ(q)вҹ©amp. . subscriptОӣО“рқ‘һрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқ‘Ҙsubscriptquantum-operator-productрқң“рқ‘һsubscriptрқ’ӘО“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІВҜрқң“рқ‘һampLambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime)=sumxpsi(q)|OGamma(x,z,y,yprime)|barpsi(q)rm amp. . (II.3.1) A summation over the position of the staple-shaped operator is taken, which is allowed by translational symmetry, in order to simplify the calculations. Such GreenвҖҷs functions using local operators are easily calculated in continuum perturbation theory to very high order. However, due to the nonlocal nature of the staple-shaped operators, additional scales (staple lengths) appear in GreenвҖҷs functions, which make the computation more complex even at the one-loop level. The corresponding calculation on the lattice is even more demanding since the procedure for isolating divergences from the Feynman integrals, as well as the procedure for taking the continuum limit aвҶ’0вҶ’рқ‘Һ0arightarrow 0 (where aрқ‘Һa is the lattice spacing) are more complicated (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108). There are four one-loop Feynman diagrams contributing to ОӣО“вҖӢ(q,z,y,yвҖІ)subscriptОӣО“рқ‘һрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІLambdaGamma(q,z,y,yprime), shown in Fig. . These diagrams will appear in both continuum and lattice regularizations since all vertices are present in both regularizations. To this perturbative order, zero (d1subscriptрқ‘‘1d1), one (d2вҲ’d3subscriptрқ‘‘2subscriptрқ‘‘3d2-d3) or two (d4subscriptрқ‘‘4d4) gluons stem from the staple-shaped Wilson line. Due to the shape of the staple, the diagrams are further divided into thirteen subdiagrams, shown in Fig. , depending on the side of the staple from which gluons emanate. content_sections.II Calculation setup II.4 Renormalizatio: We formulate below different versions of appropriate regularization-independent (RI) prescriptions which address all possible divergences and mixing of the staple-shaped operators. In contrast to our previous study regarding the renormalization of symmetric staple operators, we construct renormalization matrices that address the mixing as observed by studying symmetries and not by studying one-loop lattice perturbation theory; thus, the renormalization matrices will be 4Г—4444times 4. Several of the nondiagonal elements of these matrices will vanish at one-loop. In this way, the renormalization prescription will be more appropriate for nonperturbative calculations addressing possible mixing that can be seen in higher loops. We first give our conventions regarding the renormalization of the operators under study, as well as of the relevant elementary fields and parameters that enter our perturbative calculations: рқ’ӘО“RвҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)=ZО“вҖӢО“вҖІR,XвҖӢрқ’ӘО“вҖІXвҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ),ПҲRвҖӢ(x)=(ZПҲR,X)1/2вҖӢПҲXвҖӢ(x),gR=Ој(4вҲ’d)/2вҖӢZgR,XвҖӢgX,formulae-sequencesubscriptsuperscriptрқ’Әрқ‘…О“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқ‘ӢО“superscriptО“вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptрқ’Әрқ‘ӢsuperscriptО“вҖІрқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІformulae-sequencesuperscriptрқң“рқ‘…рқ‘Ҙsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқ‘Ӣрқң“12superscriptрқң“рқ‘Ӣрқ‘Ҙsuperscriptрқ‘”рқ‘…superscriptрқңҮ4рқ‘‘2subscriptsuperscriptрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқ‘Ӣрқ‘”superscriptрқ‘”рқ‘ӢORGamma(x,z,y,yprime)=ZR,XGammaGammaprimeOXGammaprime(x,z,y,yprime),qquadpsiR(x)=(ZR,Xpsi)1/2psiX(x),qquad gR=mu(4-d)/2ZR,XggX, (II.4.1) where ПҲXвҖӢ(ПҲR)superscriptрқң“рқ‘Ӣsuperscriptрқң“рқ‘…psiX(psiR) is the bare (renormalized) fermion field, gXвҖӢ(gR)superscriptрқ‘”рқ‘Ӣsuperscriptрқ‘”рқ‘…gX(gR) is the bare (renormalized) coupling constant. Use of these two choices of projectors amounts to the implementation of two different RIвҖІsuperscriptRIвҖІrm RIprime prescriptions, which we will denote as RI1вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptRIвҖІ1rm RIprime1, RI2вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptRIвҖІ2rm RIprime2 from now on. Compared to the first choice of projectors, the second one can further remove finite contributions of some Dirac structures, allowed by Lorentz symmetry, from the elements of the renormalization matrices. Similar projectors have also been studied in the renormalization of local operators, leading to reduced contributions from hadronic contamination in the nonperturbative data, especially for small values of the renormalization scale qВҜВҜрқ‘һbarq. The RI scheme can address the power8Some power divergences may remain beyond one loopcitebib.bib66. and logarithmic divergences, as well as the mixing between different Dirac structures, in the same way as MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS does. However, in contrast to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme, RI can also treat the pinch-pole singularity when yвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘Ұyrightarrowinfty. This means that this infinite limit can be taken only in the RI-renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions and not in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions of the staple operators. This is true because both schemes are defined for finite values of yрқ‘Ұy, where pinch-pole singularities are not present. Then, terms that diverge with yрқ‘Ұy (in the yвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘Ұyrightarrowinfty limit) do not contribute to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS renormalization function, but they do so in RI. Thus, when multiplying the bare GreenвҖҷs functions with their renormalization functions, only in RI these terms are eliminated. However, since RI is just an intermediate scheme entering the procedure of renormalizing the operators in the reference scheme MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS, nonperturbatively, we cannot benefit from this additional feature of RI; after conversion to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme, the pinch-pole singularity comes back. Thus, the standard MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS prescription is not appropriate for renormalizing staple operators in the infinite limit yвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘Ұyrightarrowinfty. вҲҷвҲҷbullet Alternative prescriptions: Alternative prescriptions which are also applicable in the limit yвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘Ұyrightarrowinfty are described below. The main feature in these prescriptions stems from the fact that the same pinch-pole singularity arises in a closed Wilson loop. Also, this object shows power and cusp divergences as the staple-shaped operators. As proposed in Ref. citebib.bib65, we can redefine the standard staple-shaped operator рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma by dividing it with the square root of the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular zГ—(y+yвҖІ)рқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІztimes(y+yprime) Wilson loop LвҖӢ(z,y+yвҖІ)рқҗҝрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІL(z,y+yprime): рқ’ӘВҜО“вҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)вүЎрқ’ӘО“вҖӢ(x,z,y,yвҖІ)вҹЁLвҖӢ(z,y+yвҖІ)вҹ©1/2,subscriptВҜрқ’ӘО“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқ’ӘО“рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsuperscriptdelimited-вҹЁвҹ©рқҗҝрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІ12overlineOGamma(x,z,y,yprime)equivfracOGamma(x,z,y,yprime) L(z,y+yprime)1/2, (II.4.7) where LвҖӢ(z,y+yвҖІ)вүЎ1NcвҖӢTrвҖӢ[рқ’«вҖӢрқ’°вҖӢ(x,(y+yвҖІ)вҖӢОҪ2)вҖӢрқ’°вҖӢ(x+(y+yвҖІ)вҖӢОҪ2,zвҖӢОҪ1)вҖӢрқ’°вҖ вҖӢ(x+zвҖӢОҪ1,(y+yвҖІ)вҖӢОҪ2)вҖӢрқ’°вҖ вҖӢ(x,zвҖӢОҪ1)]. рқҗҝрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІ1subscriptрқ‘Ғрқ‘җTrdelimited-рқ’«рқ’°рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2рқ’°рқ‘Ҙрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2рқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1superscriptрқ’°вҖ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsubscriptрқңҲ2superscriptрқ’°вҖ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§subscriptрқңҲ1L(z,y+yprime)equivfrac1Ncrm Tr[PU(x,(y+yprime)hatnu2) U(x+(y+yprime)hatnu2,zhatnu1) Udagger(x+zhatnu1,(y+yprime)hatnu2) Udagger(x,zhatnu1)]. Older studies of Wilson loops can be found, e. g. , in Refs. citebib.bib123, bib.bib128 in both continuum and lattice using Wilson gluons. Here, we extend these calculations to the case of Symanzik-improved gluons. For completeness, we have also repeated the continuum calculation. Since the end-point divergences do not depend on the dimensions of the staple10On the contrary, power divergences depend on the total length of the staple line and thus, renormalization functions in ordinary RI must be computed separately for each choice of the dimensions of the staple. , a nonperturbative determination of ZВҜО“вҖӢО“вҖІR,XsubscriptsuperscriptВҜрқ‘Қрқ‘…рқ‘ӢО“superscriptО“вҖІoverlineZR,XGammaGammaprime is expected to exhibit a much milder dependence on the staple lengths z,y,yвҖІрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІz,y,yprime which lie in the renormalization window: aвүӘzвүӘОӣQCDвҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘§much-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD1all zllLambdarm QCD-1, aвүӘyвүӘОӣQCDвҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘Һрқ‘Ұmuch-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD1all yllLambdarm QCD-1, aвүӘyвҖІвүӘОӣQCDвҲ’1much-less-thanрқ‘Һsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІmuch-less-thansuperscriptsubscriptОӣQCD1all yprimellLambdarm QCD-1. In this way it becomes more acceptable to renormalize the modified operators рқ’ӘВҜО“subscriptВҜрқ’ӘО“overlineOGamma defined at large values of the lengths z,y,yвҖІрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІz,y,yprime, using renormalization functions ZВҜО“вҖӢО“вҖІRвҖӢIвҖІ,XsubscriptsuperscriptВҜрқ‘Қрқ‘…superscriptрқҗјвҖІрқ‘ӢО“superscriptО“вҖІoverlineZRIprime,XGammaGammaprime defined at smaller values of z,y,yвҖІрқ‘§рқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІz,y,yprime within the perturbative region. To distinguish the lengths appearing in the bare operators from the reference lengths appearing in the renormalization functions, we will call the latter as zВҜ,yВҜ,yВҜвҖІВҜрқ‘§ВҜрқ‘ҰsuperscriptВҜрқ‘ҰвҖІbarz,bary,baryprime. If one is interested in taking the limit yвҶ’вҲһвҶ’рқ‘Ұyrightarrowinfty, then there are only two relevant lengths: zВҜВҜрқ‘§barz and yВҜвҲ’yВҜвҖІВҜрқ‘ҰsuperscriptВҜрқ‘ҰвҖІbary-baryprime. Another option that we do not consider in this work is the short-distance ratio (SDR) scheme, described in Ref. citebib.bib66. In this scheme, ratios of hadron matrix elements of the modified operators at different external momenta are considered. All the good features of RI-bar are also valid in the SDR scheme. However, we stress that SDR is valid when operator mixing is absent or negligible.

The different Dirac structures appearing in Eqs. ( вҖ“ ) can be classified into two categories: the structures corresponding to the tree-level GreenвҖҷs functions of the staple operators that belong to the same mixing set according to symmetries (structures multiplied by ОЈi,1subscriptОЈрқ‘–1Sigmai,1 or ОЈi,2subscriptОЈрқ‘–2Sigmai,2), and all the remaining structures. The former structures are multiplied by regularization-dependent form factors (ОЈi,jsubscriptОЈрқ‘–рқ‘—Sigmai,j), while the form factors of the latter structures do not depend on regularization. This is confirmed by comparing our one-loop results in both continuum and lattice regularizations (Eq. ). In particular, the bare GreenвҖҷs functions in the continuum consist of two instead of four structures of the first category because chiral symmetry is preserved (see discussion in Sec. ). On the other hand, the lattice bare GreenвҖҷs functions at one loop also contain a pair of the four first-category structures, which is different from that obtained in DR (see Sec. ). Since the lattice regularization that we employ breaks chiral symmetry, we expect that the missing two structures of the first category will appear in higher loops. By taking into account the classification of the Dirac structures in the GreenвҖҷs functions, we conclude that a renormalization prescription that considers a wider mixing pattern, i. e. , mixing among all 16 independent operators of different О“О“Gamma matrices, is not an optimal choice for renormalizing the staple operators. In this case, several form factors ОЈi,jsubscriptОЈрқ‘–рқ‘—Sigmai,j will contribute to the 16Г—16161616times 16 mixing matrix, including pure dependence on the regularization-independent form factors ОЈi,j>2subscriptОЈрқ‘–рқ‘—2Sigmai,j>2 in some nondiagonal elements. Given that we are interested in matching bare lattice GreenвҖҷs functions to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme (through an intermediate nonperturbative scheme), in which the regularization-independent form factors cannot contribute, it is more economic to consider an intermediate renormalization prescription that includes only the minimum number of operators necessary for disentangling the mixing occurring in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme on the lattice. In our study, we consider such a minimal set of operators as dictated by symmetries. The one-loop renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions in the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme can be obtained by removing the рқ’ӘвҖӢ(1/Оө)рқ’Ә1рқңҖO(1/varepsilon) term in Eq. . Also, the renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions in any variant of the RI scheme can be obtained by imposing the corresponding renormalization conditions to the above results. The extraction of the conversion functions among these schemes (RI variants and MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS) is then straightforward; they are given in Eqs. (, вҖ“ ) for the schemes under study. (III.3.38) Another property of the conversion matrices comes from the combination of рқ’«Ојsubscriptрқ’«рқңҮPmu, рқ’ҜОј,рқ’һsubscriptрқ’ҜрқңҮрқ’һTmu,C symmetries: one can prove (see Eq. ) that the real (imaginary) parts of the conversion matrices are even (odd) under (zвҶ’вҲ’zвҶ’рқ‘§рқ‘§zrightarrow-z, yвҶ’вҲ’yвҶ’рқ‘Ұрқ‘Ұyrightarrow-y, yвҖІвҶ’вҲ’yвҖІвҶ’superscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІyprimerightarrow-yprime). This is confirmed by our one-loop computation. We illustrate our results for the conversion matrices in the plots of Figs. ( вҖ“ ) by employing certain values of the free parameters used in lattice simulations: ОјВҜ=2ВҜрқңҮ2barmu=2 GeV, ОІ=1рқӣҪ1beta=1 (Landau gauge), aвҖӢqВҜ=(2вҖӢПҖLвҖӢn1,2вҖӢПҖLвҖӢn2,2вҖӢПҖLвҖӢn3,2вҖӢПҖTвҖӢ(n4+12))рқ‘ҺВҜрқ‘һ2рқңӢрқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘ӣ12рқңӢрқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘ӣ22рқңӢрқҗҝsubscriptрқ‘ӣ32рқңӢрқ‘Үsubscriptрқ‘ӣ412abarq=(frac2piLn1,frac2piLn2,frac2piLn3,frac2piT(n4+frac12)), where aрқ‘Һa is the lattice spacing, L3Г—Tsuperscriptрқҗҝ3рқ‘ҮL3times T is the lattice volume and niвҲҲв„Өsubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘–в„ӨniinZ. Following simulations by ETMC, we choose isotropic momentum scales in the spatial directions (n1=n2=n3subscriptрқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқ‘ӣ2subscriptрқ‘ӣ3n1=n2=n3) and a nonzero twist of 1/2121/2 in the temporal component; the latter choice is compatible with the antiperiodic boundary conditions applied on the fermion fields in the temporal direction. As an example, we apply n1=n2=n3=4subscriptрқ‘ӣ1subscriptрқ‘ӣ2subscriptрқ‘ӣ34n1=n2=n3=4, n4=5subscriptрқ‘ӣ45n4=5, L=32рқҗҝ32L=32, T=64рқ‘Ү64T=64 and a=0.09рқ‘Һ0.09a=0.09 fm. We note that specific choices of nisubscriptрқ‘ӣрқ‘–ni can lead to a vanishing imaginary part. In particular, by setting to zero the two momentum components parallel to the staple segments, the one-loop expression for the conversion matrices becomes purely real. However, such a choice of momentum gives rise to unwanted Lorentz noninvariant contributions in the nonperturbative calculations. Thus, in our example, we follow the common practice of employing democratic momenta with reduced Lorentz noninvariant contributions at the cost of introducing an imaginary part in the RI-type renormalization matrices and the conversion matrices. In Figs. ( вҖ“ ), we examine the dependence of some representative conversion matrix elements on the Wilson-line scales yрқ‘Ұy, zрқ‘§z, and yвҲ’yвҖІрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІy-yprime (in lattice units), respectively. In particular, we plot the real and imaginary parts of the 2Г—2222times 2 block CО“вҖӢО“вҖІMSВҜ,RIвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptрқҗ¶ВҜMSsuperscriptRIвҖІО“superscriptО“вҖІCoverlinerm MS,rm RIprimeGammaGammaprime, (О“,О“вҖІ=ОіОҪ1,ОіОҪ2)formulae-sequenceО“superscriptО“вҖІsubscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ1subscriptрқӣҫsubscriptрқңҲ2(Gamma,Gammaprime=gammanu1,gammanu2), for the four renormalization prescriptions: RI1вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ1prime1, RI2вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ2prime2, RI1вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ1prime1-bar, RI2вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ2prime2-bar. As shown in Fig. , the real part of the diagonal elements (first plot in the left column and second plot in the right column) has an almost linear (flat) dependence on y/aрқ‘Ұрқ‘Һy/a in the RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei (RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei-bar) schemes due to the presence (absence) of the pinch-pole singularity. As expected, the convergent behavior of RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei-bar appears for large values of y/aрқ‘Ұрқ‘Һy/a (вүі6greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent6gtrsim 6). There are no significant differences in the real diagonal elements between schemes with index 1 and 2. The real part of the nondiagonal elements (first plot in the second column and second plot in the first column of Fig. ) also converges for large values of y/aрқ‘Ұрқ‘Һy/a (вүі8greater-than-or-equivalent-toabsent8gtrsim 8) for all RI-type schemes. Note that the nondiagonal elements are identical between RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei and RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei-bar schemes (for the same iрқ‘–i) at one loop. Differences between schemes with index 1 and 2 are now visible: the real nondiagonal elements for the RI2вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ2prime2 scheme have smaller absolute values compared to RI1вҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІ1prime1, which are closer to zero. The relative size of the real nondiagonal elements compared to the diagonal ones is вҲј20%similar-toabsentpercent20sim 20%. The imaginary part of the conversion matrix elements (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ) gives a much milder contribution compared to the real one (вүІ10%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent10lesssim 10%). The imaginary (diagonal and nondiagonal) elements for RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei-bar coincide with the corresponding elements for RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei (for the same iрқ‘–i) at one loop. A plateau is observed at large values of y/aрқ‘Ұрқ‘Һy/a; a more stable behavior is seen for the nondiagonal elements. As in the case of real nondiagonal elements, noticeable distinctions can also be spotted in the imaginary parts between schemes with index 1 and 2. However, there is no consistent pattern regarding which scheme yields smaller contributions, as it varies for each element. Similar conclusions are extracted from Fig. by considering the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on z/aрқ‘§рқ‘Һz/a. Here, we observe a convergent dependence for z/aвүі6greater-than-or-equivalent-toрқ‘§рқ‘Һ6z/agtrsim 6 for all conversion matrix elements, except for the imaginary diagonal parts (the four plots in the last two rows of Fig. ). Now, the real diagonal parts have flat behavior in both RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei and RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei-bar schemes since the pinch-pole singularity does not arise for large values of z/aрқ‘§рқ‘Һz/a. Examining the dependence of the conversion matrix elements on (yвҲ’yвҖІ)/aрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқ‘Һ(y-yprime)/a in Fig. , we conclude that a more flat behavior is observed for smaller values of (yвҲ’yвҖІ)/aрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқ‘Һ(y-yprime)/a, while for larger values the elements decrease (in most cases) rapidly. An almost linear dependence on (yвҲ’yвҖІ)/aрқ‘Ұsuperscriptрқ‘ҰвҖІрқ‘Һ(y-yprime)/a with a negative slope is obtained in the real diagonal parts of the RIiвҖІsubscriptsuperscriptabsentвҖІрқ‘–primei schemes (see the first plot in the first column and the second plot in the second column of Fig. ), coming from the pinch-pole divergent term. In summary, the conversion matrices exhibit significant contributions from the real diagonal components, whereas the imaginary diagonal and nondiagonal elements make comparatively milder yet perceptible contributions. Safer conclusions by comparing the different types of renormalization schemes can be obtained when combining the conversion matrices with the nonperturbative data.

We selected three of the most common choices of the Symanzik coefficients cisubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘–ci, called Wilson, Tree-Level Symanzik, and Iwasaki gluon actions, as shown in Table . Since we consider mass-independent renormalization schemes, we set the quark mass equal to zero. Consequently, the results from this study are also applicable to the twisted mass fermions citebib.bib131 in the chiral limit. One should, however, keep in mind that, in going from the twisted basis to the physical basis, operator identifications are modified (e. g. , the scalar density, under вҖңmaximal twistвҖқ, turns into a pseudoscalar density, etc. ). The results for the one-loop lattice bare GreenвҖҷs functions of the asymmetric staple operators ОӣО“LRsuperscriptsubscriptОӣО“LRLambdaGammarm LR are presented below in terms of the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions, derived by the corresponding calculation in DR. The methodology for calculating the one-loop momentum integrals on the lattice is described in Refs. citebib.bib105, bib.bib108. Here, we cite results for a general Wilson-line lattice operator with nрқ‘ӣn cusps [(n+1)рқ‘ӣ1(n+1) segments] and no self-intersections, as calculated in our previous study considering symmetric staplescitebib.bib108. We confirm that the formula constructed in the latter publication gives the correct result for the difference between the bare lattice GreenвҖҷs functions and the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized GreenвҖҷs functions, also in the case of asymmetric staple operators studied in this work. The corresponding MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized GreenвҖҷs function ОӣО“MSВҜsuperscriptsubscriptОӣО“ВҜMSLambdaGammaoverlinerm MS can be read from Eqs. ( вҖ“ ) by removing 1/Оө1рқңҖ1/varepsilon terms. Conclusions from this calculation are summarized below: 1. Linear divergence: On the lattice, there is a linear divergence, which depends on the length of the Wilson line and the gluon action that is employed. This divergence comes from diagrams contributing to the Wilson-line self-energy. At one loop, the contributing diagram is only d4subscriptрқ‘‘4d4. 2. Logarithmic divergences: In both regularizations (DR and lattice), there are end-point logarithmic divergences, coming from diagrams d2subscriptрқ‘‘2d2 and d3subscriptрқ‘‘3d3, and cusp and contact logarithmic divergences, coming from diagram d4subscriptрқ‘‘4d4. The coefficients in front of these divergences are regularization-independent. 3. Operator mixing: Diagrams d2subscriptрқ‘‘2d2 and d3subscriptрқ‘‘3d3 give rise (upon summation) to the Dirac structure О“вҖІ=(О“вҖӢОҪМёi+ОҪМёfвҖӢО“)/2superscriptО“вҖІО“subscriptitalic-ОҪМёрқ‘–subscriptitalic-ОҪМёрқ‘“О“2Gammaprime=(Gammahatnotnui+hatnotnufGamma)/2, which differs from the tree-level structure О“О“Gamma of the operator. This indicates that operator mixing is present: In order to remove this additional structure, we need to renormalize the Wilson-line operators рқ’ӘО“subscriptрқ’ӘО“OGamma and рқ’ӘО“вҖІsubscriptрқ’ӘsuperscriptО“вҖІOGammaprime as a doublet by introducing a 2Г—2222times 2 mixing renormalization matrix. However, as concluded by symmetries, the employment of 4Г—4444times 4 mixing matrices for renormalizing quadruplets of asymmetric staple-shaped operators is expected to be required at higher loops. The mixing contributions at one loop depend solely on the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints, regardless of the shape of the Wilson line. Also, the coefficient Оұ2+Оұ3вҖӢcSWsubscriptрқӣј2subscriptрқӣј3subscriptрқ‘җSWalpha2+alpha3 crm SW in front of the structure О“вҖІsuperscriptО“вҖІGammaprime depends on рқ“Үрқ“Үr and cSвҖӢWsubscriptрқ‘җрқ‘Ҷрқ‘ҠcSW; in particular, Оұ2subscriptрқӣј2alpha2 vanishes when рқ“Ү=0рқ“Ү0r=0. Thus, the one-loop mixing contributions originate from the chirality-breaking parts of the fermion action. As concluded by symmetries, these specific contributions are expected to be absent when a chiral-fermion action is employed. 4. Finite contributions: Diagram d1subscriptрқ‘‘1d1 is identical in DR and lattice regularization (up to discretization effects рқ’ӘвҖӢ(a)рқ’Әрқ‘ҺO(a)) giving no contribution to the difference ОҙвҖӢОӣО“рқӣҝsubscriptОӣО“deltaLambdaGamma. Finite contributions of diagrams d2subscriptрқ‘‘2d2 and d3subscriptрқ‘‘3d3 stem only from the endpoints of the Wilson line. Any parts of a segment that do not include the endpoints give finite contributions which differ between DR and lattice regularization only by discretization effects. In diagram d4subscriptрқ‘‘4d4, the finite contributions come from the cusps and the straight-line segments of the Wilson line; they depend on both the number of cusps (nрқ‘ӣn) and the number of segments (n+1рқ‘ӣ1n+1). Hence, the exact shape of the Wilson line does not affect the one-loop continuum and lattice GreenвҖҷs functions of the Wilson-line operators differently; the only additional contributions on the lattice depend on the total length, the number of cusps, and the direction of the Wilson line entering the endpoints. content_sections.V Summary and Future plans: In this work, we present an extensive and comprehensive study of the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear operators featuring an asymmetric staple-shaped Wilson line. This project is motivated by the increased interest in studying TMDPDFs from lattice QCD using novel approaches, such as large momentum effective theory and short-distance factorization, which require matrix elements of the operators under study. More details can be found in the TMD Handbookcitebib.bib133. The analysis is based on a one-loop perturbative calculation of GreenвҖҷs functions of such operators in both lattice and continuum (dimensional) regularizations. Based on our results, we identify the mixing pattern of these operators and propose renormalization prescriptions applicable to perturbative and nonperturbative data. More precisely, we discuss RI-type conditions by using different projectors that effectively address power and logarithmic divergences, as well as the finite mixing among operators with different Dirac structures. We have systematically analyzed the mixing patterns of these operators, leveraging symmetry arguments for both chiral and nonchiral fermions. We also introduce a variant of the RI prescription, which removes the pinch-pole singularities inherent in staple operators of infinite length by incorporating rectangular Wilson loops. This strategy also eliminates residual power divergences. Another novel aspect of this work is the extraction of the conversion factors to the MSВҜВҜMSoverlinerm MS scheme using the results in dimensional regularization. Our calculations are performed for arbitrary values of the renormalization momentum scale and the spatial dimensions of the staple. This ensures their applicability across a broad spectrum of nonperturbative investigations that may use the results of this work. Potential future work includes an ambitious extension of this calculation to two-loop perturbation theory. This direction has the potential to offer valuable insights and improvements to ongoing nonperturbative investigations. By performing higher-order loop calculations, we aim to refine the renormalization procedure and eliminate sources of systematic uncertainties. Additionally, we plan to calculate one-loop lattice discretization effects across a range of staple lengths and momentum scales, which has significant potential for enhancing nonperturbative estimates, particularly at short distances. Other possible directions include calculations with different lattice formulations, which might be interesting for the TMD community. Acknowledgements. G. S. and H. P. acknowledge financial support from the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation under contract number EXCELLENCE/0421/0025. M. C. acknowledges financial support from the U. S. Department of Energy, Office of Nuclear Physics, Early Career Award under Grant No. DE-SC0020405. M. C. is grateful for the hospitality of the University of Cyprus during the completion of this manuscript under the project 3D-nucleon, ID number EXCELLENCE/0421/0043, co-financed by the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research and Innovation Foundation.

sorted_papers.title:
Renormalization of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution on
  the lattice
sorted_papers.abstract:
To calculate the transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions
(TMDPDFs) from lattice QCD, an important goal yet to be realized, it is crucial
to establish a viable non-perturbative renormalization approach for linear
divergences in the corresponding Euclidean quasi-TMDPDF correlators in
large-momentum effective theory. We perform a first systematic study of the
renormalization property of the quasi-TMDPDFs by calculating the relevant
matrix elements in a pion state at 5 lattice spacings ranging from 0.03 fm to
0.12 fm. We demonstrate that the square root of the Wilson loop combined with
the short distance hadron matrix element provides a successful method to remove
all ultraviolet divergences of the quasi-TMD operator, and thus provide the
necessary justification to perform a continuum limit calculation of TMDPDFs. In
contrast, the popular RI/MOM renormalization scheme fails to eliminate all
linear divergences.

qualitative: The square root of the Wilson loop combined with the short-distance hadron matrix element successfully removes all ultraviolet divergences in the quasi-TMDPDF operator, providing a viable non-perturbative renormalization method for transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions in lattice QCD.
The RI/MOM renormalization scheme fails to eliminate all linear divergences in the quasi-TMDPDF correlators, making it unsuitable for this purpose.

The renormalization procedure relies on the subtraction of the Wilson loop factor, which captures the long-distance soft contributions and cancels the linear divergences arising from the staple-shaped gauge link.
The continuum limit of the quasi-TMDPDF can be reliably performed only after applying the Wilson-loop-based renormalization, which ensures the removal of both UV and linear divergences.
The success of the Wilson-loop-based method is demonstrated through a systematic study across five lattice spacings, confirming its consistency and applicability in the continuum limit.
quantitative: h(b,z,p_z) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{\text{spin}} \langle q(p) | \bar{q} \mathcal{W}(b,z,L) \gamma^t q(b,z) | q(p) \rangle

At zero momentum and in the limit $ L \gg b,z $, the renormalized quasi-TMDPDF is given by: $ \tilde{h}^{\pi,\gamma^t}(b,z,0) \equiv \frac{\tilde{h}^{\pi,\gamma^t}(b,z,L,0)}{\sqrt{Z_E(b,2L+z)}} = 1 + \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} + \gamma_E + \ln \pi\right) + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{3}{2} \ln[\mu^2(b^2 + z^2)] - 2 \frac{z}{b} \arctan\left(\frac{z}{b}\right) \right] + \mathcal{O}(\alpha_s^2) $

The 1-loop Wilson loop factor is: $ Z_E(b,2L+z) = \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{\pi} \left[ 2\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} + \gamma_E + \ln \pi\right) + 4 + 2 \ln(\mu^2 b^2) + \frac{\pi}{2} \frac{L+z}{b} \right] + \mathcal{O}(\alpha_s^2) $

In the limit $ L \gg b,z $, the Wilson loop simplifies to: $ Z_E \to \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{\pi} \left[ 2\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon} + \gamma_E + \ln \pi\right) + 2 + 2 \ln(\mu^2 b^2) + \frac{\pi}{2} \frac{L+z}{b} \right] + \mathcal{O}(\alpha_s^2) $

The divergent parts of the vertex, staple, and tadpole diagrams at zero momentum are: $ \tilde{h}_{\text{vertex}} = \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{4\pi} \left( -\frac{1}{\epsilon} - \ln[\mu^2(b^2 + z^2)] + 1 - \gamma_E - \ln \pi \right) $, $ \tilde{h}_{\text{sail}} = \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{2\pi} \left( \frac{1}{\epsilon} + \ln[\mu^2(b^2 + z^2)] + \gamma_E + \ln \pi \right) $, $ \tilde{h}_{\text{tadpole}} = \frac{\alpha_s C_f}{2\pi} \left[ 3\left( \frac{1}{\epsilon} + \ln \pi + \gamma_E + 2 \right) + \ln\left(\frac{\mu^2 L^2}{b^2} \right) + \ln\left(\frac{\mu^2 (L+z)^2}{b^2} \right) + 2 \frac{L}{b} \arctan\left(\frac{L}{b}\right) + 2 \frac{L+z}{b} \arctan\left(\frac{L+z}{b}\right) - 2 \frac{z}{b} \arctan\left(\frac{z}{b}\right) - \ln\left( \frac{(b^2 + L^2)[b^2 + (L+z)^2]}{b^2(b^2 + z^2)} \right) \right] $

content_sections.Supplemental Materials .1 Perturbative c: For perturbative calculations, we can replace the external state of the matrix element with an on-shell quark state. We thus have hвҖӢ(b,z,pz)=12вҖӢвҲ‘sвҖӢpвҖӢiвҖӢnвҖӢsвҹЁqвҖӢ(p)|qВҜвҖӢвҖӢрқ’ІвҖӢ(b,z,L)вҖӢОіtвҖӢqвҖӢ(b,z)|qвҖӢ(p)вҹ©в„Һрқ‘Ҹрқ‘§subscriptрқ‘қрқ‘§12subscriptрқ‘ рқ‘қрқ‘–рқ‘ӣрқ‘ quantum-operator-productрқ‘һрқ‘қВҜрқ‘һ0рқ’Ірқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқҗҝsuperscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘һрқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқ‘һрқ‘қdisplaystyletildeh(b,z,pz)=frac12sumspins q(p)|barqcal W(b,z,L)gammatq(b,z)|q(p) . For the renormalization and matching in the SDR scheme, we need to calculate the above matrix element at zero momentum. This can be done in analogy with the calculation in Ref. citebib.bib24. content_sections.Supplemental Materials .2 Wilson loop ex: ZEвҖӢ(b,r;a)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘ҺZE(b,r;a) is the vacuum expectation value of a rectangular spacelike Wilson loop of different lattice spacings with size bГ—rрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹbtimes r, rвүЎ2вҖӢL+zрқ‘ҹ2рқҗҝрқ‘§requiv 2L+z. And we need to take the square root of ZEвҖӢ(b,r;a)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘ҺZE(b,r;a) to cancel out the transverse link, pinch-pole singularity and linear divergence in hв„Һtildeh defined in Eq. . But the signal to noise ratio (SNR) of ZEвҖӢ(b,r;a)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘ҺZE(b,r;a) can be very poor at large bрқ‘Ҹb and/or rрқ‘ҹr as shown in Fig. . The central value of ZEвҖӢ(b,r)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹZE(b,r) with finite statistics can even give a negative value and make ZEвҖӢ(b,r)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹsqrtZE(b,r) to be ill-defined. But since ZEвҖӢ(b,r)rвҶ’вҲһвҶ’вҖӢCвҖӢ(b)вҖӢeвҲ’VвҖӢ(b)вҖӢrsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёsubscriptрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹвҶ’вҶ’рқ‘ҹрқҗ¶рқ‘Ҹsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘үрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹZE(b,r)overrightarrowrrightarrowinftyC(b)e-V(b)r where VвҖӢ(b)рқ‘үрқ‘ҸV(b) is the QCD static potential, we can fit ZEвҖӢ(b,r;a)subscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘ҺZE(b,r;a) by ecвҖӢ(b;a)вҲ’VвҖӢ(b;a)вҖӢrsuperscriptрқ‘’рқ‘җрқ‘Ҹрқ‘Һрқ‘үрқ‘Ҹрқ‘Һрқ‘ҹec(b;a)-V(b;a)r at large rрқ‘ҹr and replace it with the fit result. As shown in Fig, the effective mass of Wilson loop meffвҖӢ(b,r;a)=lnвҖӢZEвҖӢ(b,r;a)ZEвҖӢ(b,r+1;a)subscriptрқ‘ҡeffрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘Һlnsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹрқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ҹ1рқ‘Һmtexteff(b,r;a)=textlnfracZE(b,r;a)ZE(b,r+1;a) will saturate to a plateau at large rрқ‘ҹr, and the plateau will be shorter for smaller lattice spacing and larger bрқ‘Ҹb. For the fit window, we define the threshold rmaxsubscriptрқ‘ҹmaxrtextmax as the point where SNR is more than 3, and our fitting range is chosen to be rmax20.7вҖӢrmaxрқ‘ҹ0.7subscriptрқ‘ҹmaxr>0.7rtextmax. We fit the Wilson loop for every sample, and the statistical uncertainty comes from bootstrap re-sampling. As shown in Fig. , such a extrapolation is important to access the large bрқ‘Ҹb and zрқ‘§z region, especially for small lattice spacing.For a well-defined quasi-TMD operator, LрқҗҝL should be large enough to ensure that the longitudinal link can extend outside the region of the parton and independent of LрқҗҝL after proper subtraction. As shown in Fig. , the matrix elements hПҖ,Оіtsubscriptв„ҺрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўhpi,gammat all show a plateau at large LрқҗҝL. To balance the statistical uncertainty and the systematic uncertainty, we take L=6вҖӢaрқҗҝ6рқ‘ҺL=6a for MILC12, L=8вҖӢaрқҗҝ8рқ‘ҺL=8a for MILC09, L=11вҖӢaрқҗҝ11рқ‘ҺL=11a for MILC06, L=13вҖӢaрқҗҝ13рқ‘ҺL=13a for MILC04, and L=14вҖӢaрқҗҝ14рқ‘ҺL=14a for MILC03 in most cases. For the cases with larger bрқ‘Ҹb on the MILC03 ensemble, we set L=bрқҗҝрқ‘ҸL=b (more specifically, we set L=16вҖӢaрқҗҝ16рқ‘ҺL=16a for b=16вҖӢaрқ‘Ҹ16рқ‘Һb=16a, and L=18вҖӢaрқҗҝ18рқ‘ҺL=18a for b=18вҖӢaрқ‘Ҹ18рқ‘Һb=18a). On the other hand, the LрқҗҝL dependence saturation in the renormalized matrix elements in RI/MOM scheme hПҖ,ОіtMOMsubscriptsuperscriptв„ҺMOMрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘ЎhtextMOMpi,gammat is better than hПҖ,Оіtsubscriptв„ҺрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўhpi,gammat as shown in Fig. , while the residual linear divergence is obvious. Thus practically, we take Lвүғ0.36similar-to-or-equalsрқҗҝ0.36Lsimeq 0.36fm for b=0.12рқ‘Ҹ0.12b=0.12fm in the RI/MOM case to make the comparison.
content_sections.Supplemental Materials .3 Action depende: As shown in Fig. , the bare pion matrix elements hПҖ,ОіtвҖӢ(b,z,L,0;1/a)subscriptв„ҺрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқҗҝ01рқ‘Һtildehpi,gammat(b,z,L,0;1/a) with clover valence quark and overlap valence quark are consistent with each other for different bрқ‘Ҹb and zрқ‘§z at large LрқҗҝL. Since Wilson loop wonвҖҷt be affected by the valence quark, the matrix elements hПҖ,ОіtвҖӢ(b,z,0;1/a)вүЎhПҖ,ОіtвҖӢ(b,z,L,0;1/a)/ZEвҖӢ(b,2вҖӢL+z;1/a)subscriptв„ҺрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҹрқ‘§01рқ‘Һsubscriptв„ҺрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўрқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқҗҝ01рқ‘Һsubscriptрқ‘Қрқҗёрқ‘Ҹ2рқҗҝрқ‘§1рқ‘Һhpi,gammat(b,z,0;1/a)equivtildehpi,gammat(b,z,L,0;1/a)/sqrtZE(b,2L+z;1/a) of the overlap fermion are also almost the same as that using the clover fermion. This conclusion is the same as that in the straight link casecitebib.bib21. But as shown in Fig. , the quark matrix elements of clover valence quark and overlap valence quark are quite different from each other, and make hПҖ,ОіtMOMsubscriptsuperscriptв„ҺMOMрқңӢsubscriptрқӣҫрқ‘Ўhrm MOMpi,gammat to be very sensitive to the quark action.In order to estimate the operator-mixing effects, we adopt the similar method to Refs. citebib.bib30 and calculate the nonperturbative RI/MOM renormalization/mixing factors, в„ірқ’«вҖӢО“вҖӢ(b,z;1/a)=AbsвҖӢ[TrвҖӢ[рқ’«вҖӢвҹЁqвҖӢ(p)|qВҜвҖӢО“вҖӢрқ’ІвҖӢ(b,z,L)вҖӢq|qвҖӢ(p)вҹ©]]AbsвҖӢ[TrвҖӢ[О“вҖӢвҹЁqвҖӢ(p)|qВҜвҖӢО“вҖӢрқ’ІвҖӢ(b,z,L)вҖӢq|qвҖӢ(p)вҹ©]]subscriptв„ірқ’«О“рқ‘Ҹрқ‘§1рқ‘ҺAbsdelimited-Trdelimited-рқ’«quantum-operator-productрқ‘һрқ‘қВҜрқ‘һО“рқ’Ірқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқҗҝрқ‘һрқ‘һрқ‘қAbsdelimited-Trdelimited-О“quantum-operator-productрқ‘һрқ‘қВҜрқ‘һО“рқ’Ірқ‘Ҹрқ‘§рқҗҝрқ‘һрқ‘һрқ‘қdisplaystylecal Mcal PGamma(b,z;1/a)=fractextrmAbsleft[textrmTrleft[cal PBigq(p)Big|overlineqGammacal W(b,z,L)qBig|q(p)Bigright]right]textrmAbsleft[textrmTrleft[GammaBigq(p)Big|overlineqGammacal W(b,z,L)qBig|q(p)Bigright]right] As shown in Fig. , the mixing becomes larger with the increase of bрқ‘Ҹb and zрқ‘§z, and the decrease of aрқ‘Һa. This indicates that the mixing does not have a good continuum limit and is hard to deal with if we want to perform high precision calculation in the future. Comparing to the clover case, the mixing pattern is more regular for the overlap case as shown in Fig. . Some area becomes smaller while other area becomes larger. It is quite an interesting result since the bare hadron matrix element seems to not be affected by the valence fermion action, and the same feature has been observed in the straight link casecitebib.bib21. It is well-known that overlap fermion has a much better preservation of the chiral symmetry, thus we would like to guess that the explicit chiral symmetry of the clover fermion plays an important role to make the mixing with two quark actions to be very different. Compared to the Refs. citebib.bib30, our results are somehow different. We think the difference can come from the following reasons (from most important to least important): 1) Their external momentum has non-zero value in every direction, while our momenta is perpendicular to the staple-shaped link. We think much of the mixing comes from off-shell momentum. 2) The smearing on the gauge link used by two works are not the same, as they use Wilson flow with flow-time t=1.0рқ‘Ў1.0t=1.0, and we use 1-step HYP smearing; 3) They used the averaged diagonal matrix element as the denominator in the definition of в„ірқ’«вҖӢО“subscriptв„ірқ’«О“cal Mcal PGamma which makes the normalization to be slightly different from ours. 4) They use quenched QCD gauge field ensembles and we use the dynamic ensembles. In Fig. , we also compare the RI/MOM renormalized result with (left panel) and that ignoring the operator mixing (right panel). As shown in the figure, the mixing has little effect on residual linear divergence.

As shown in Fig. , the perturbative results cannot describe the lattice results for large bрқ‘Ҹb even for small zрқ‘§z, as a large bрқ‘Ҹb (and then a small intrinsic scale) can invalidate perturbative theory of QCD. all_papers.title: Leading Power Accuracy in Lattice Calculations of Parton Distributions
all_papers.abstract:
In lattice-QCD calculations of parton distribution functions (PDFs) via
large-momentum effective theory, the leading power (twist-three) correction
appears as ${\cal O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$ due to the linear-divergent
self-energy of Wilson line in quasi-PDF operators. For lattice data with hadron
momentum $P^z$ of a few GeV, this correction is dominant in matching, as large
as 30\% or more. We show how to eliminate this uncertainty through choosing the
mass renormalization parameter consistently with the resummation scheme of the
infrared-renormalon series in perturbative matching coefficients. An example on
the lattice pion PDF data at $P^z = 1.9$ GeV shows an improvement of matching
accuracy by a factor of more than $3\sim 5$ in the expansion region $x= 0.2\sim
0.5$.

qualitative: The leading power (twist-three) correction in lattice-QCD calculations of parton distribution functions arises from the linear-divergent self-energy of the Wilson line in quasi-PDF operators.
This correction scales as $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$ and dominates the matching uncertainty for hadron momenta $P^z$ of a few GeV.

The uncertainty from the linear divergence can be eliminated by choosing the mass renormalization parameter consistently with the resummation scheme of the infrared-renormalon series in the perturbative matching coefficients.

Wilson-loop based renormalization is essential for handling the infrared divergences associated with the Wilson line, and proper matching requires consistency between the renormalization scheme and the resummation of infrared-renormalon contributions.
quantitative: $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$: leading power correction scaling, with $\Lambda_{\rm QCD} \sim 0.2$ GeV and $P^z \sim 1.9$ GeV leading to corrections of 30% or more.
$P^z = 1.9$ GeV: specific lattice momentum used in the example calculation.
Improvement in matching accuracy by a factor of more than $3 \sim 5$ in the region $x = 0.2 \sim 0.5$ when the consistent renormalization scheme is applied.

all_papers.title: Self-Renormalization of Quasi-Light-Front Correlators on the Lattice
all_papers.abstract:
In applying large-momentum effective theory, renormalization of the Euclidean
correlators in lattice regularization is a challenge due to linear divergences
in the self-energy of Wilson lines. Based on lattice QCD matrix elements of the
quasi-PDF operator at lattice spacing $a$= 0.03 fm $\sim$ 0.12 fm with clover
and overlap valence quarks on staggered and domain-wall sea, we design a
strategy to disentangle the divergent renormalization factors from finite
physics matrix elements, which can be matched to a continuum scheme at short
distance such as dimensional regularization and minimal subtraction. Our
results indicate that the renormalization factors are universal in the hadron
state matrix elements. Moreover, the physical matrix elements appear
independent of the valence fermion formulations. These conclusions remain valid
even with HYP smearing which reduces the statistical errors albeit reducing
control of the renormalization procedure. Moreover, we find a large
non-perturbative effect in the popular RI/MOM and ratio renormalization scheme,
suggesting favor of the hybrid renormalization procedure proposed recently.

qualitative: Linear divergences in the self-energy of Wilson lines pose a significant challenge for renormalization of Euclidean correlators in lattice QCD within large-momentum effective theory.
The renormalization factors for quasi-PDF operators are universal across different hadron state matrix elements, indicating a non-perturbative universality property.

Physical matrix elements of quasi-PDF operators are independent of the valence fermion formulation (clover vs. overlap), suggesting robustness of the physical results under different discretization schemes.
HYP smearing reduces statistical errors but compromises control over the renormalization procedure, highlighting a trade-off between precision and renormalization fidelity.
The RI/MOM and ratio renormalization schemes exhibit large non-perturbative effects, indicating their limitations in accurately capturing the renormalization of quasi-PDFs.

A hybrid renormalization procedure is favored due to its ability to better handle non-perturbative effects and provide a more reliable matching to continuum schemes like dimensional regularization and minimal subtraction.
quantitative: Lattice spacing range: $ a = 0.03 \, \text{fm} \sim 0.12 \, \text{fm} $
Valence quarks: clover and overlap fermions
Sea quarks: staggered and domain-wall fermions
The renormalization factors are found to be universal across hadron states and independent of valence fermion formulation
Non-perturbative effects in RI/MOM and ratio schemes are large, suggesting the need for alternative renormalization strategies
Hybrid renormalization procedure is proposed and favored for its improved handling of non-perturbative contributions
